512	Thought for a few seconds
513	Below is a source packet for an AI coding agent. It is intentionally written as detailed input material, not as final architecture.
514	
515	Tuplet3D Vision Capture Packet
516	1. Working concept
517	Tuplet3D is a derived language and tool environment based on Tuplet.
518	
519	Tuplet currently explores:
520	
521	COR24-oriented experimental language design.
522	
523	First-class named tuples.
524	
525	User-minted verbs.
526	
527	2D whitespace/spatial parsing.
528	
529	OCaml parsing.
530	
531	Compilation/transpilation to Forth.
532	
533	Tuplet3D extends this idea into a spatial programming environment where source forms, definitions, aspects, pipelines, generated code, debugging views, and semantic projections can all be represented in 3D or higher-dimensional abstract space.
534	
535	The key distinction:
536	
537	Tuplet:
538	  Meaning emerges from 2D textual/spatial layout.
539	
540	Tuplet3D:
541	  Meaning emerges from 3D/higher-dimensional projected forms,
542	  where each view reveals a semantic aspect of the same minted verb.
543	2. Core source model
544	A Tuplet3D source object is not just a text file.
545	
546	It is a collection of positioned symbols:
547	
548	symbol {
549	  text: glyph | word | operator | minted verb | annotation
550	  coordinate: x, y, z
551	  optional_aspect_coordinate: a0, a1, a2, ...
552	  metadata
553	}
554	A definition is a bounded spatial object.
555	
556	A minted verb is the callable/semantic entity created by a definition.
557	
558	The definition may be viewed from multiple directions or projected through different aspect mappings.
559	
560	3. Standard semantic views
561	The first standard six views are:
562	
563	View	Meaning
564	Front	Algorithm gist / essential readable story
565	Left	Inputs, arity, parameter names, input types
566	Right	Outputs, result names, output types
567	Top	Preconditions
568	Bottom	Postconditions
569	Rear	Expansion / generated form / transpiled output
570	This is better than treating views as arbitrary faces. Each face has a job.
571	
572	Example
573	For a Power minted verb:
574	
575	View	Example content
576	Front	“start with 1; multiply by n, e times”
577	Left	n : ℤ, e : ℤ
578	Right	p : ℤ
579	Top	e ≥ 0
580	Bottom	p = n^e
581	Rear	Forth, assembler, Lisp, or macro-expanded Tuplet
582	4. Not limited to six views
583	Tuplet3D should not assume only six semantic aspects.
584	
585	The six physical faces are only a projection device.
586	
587	There may be many aspects:
588	
589	inputs
590	outputs
591	types
592	preconditions
593	postconditions
594	invariants
595	error handling
596	macro expansion
597	instrumentation
598	debug hooks
599	tests
600	examples
601	contracts
602	cost model
603	memory behavior
604	stack effects
605	dataflow
606	control flow
607	generated Forth
608	generated COR24 assembler
609	generated Lisp
610	documentation
611	visual explanation
612	These can be mapped into six currently visible faces.
613	
614	This is analogous to choosing a projection of higher-dimensional data.
615	
616	The UI should eventually support:
617	
618	many semantic aspects
619	  → select/relevance-rank/project
620	  → show as six 3D faces
621	  → or show as multiple 2D panels
622	5. Aspect-oriented programming connection
623	Tuplet3D has a natural relationship to aspect-oriented programming.
624	
625	A definition’s core algorithm may be visible in the front view, while other aspects are spatially attached but visually separable:
626	
627	front:
628	  algorithm gist
629	
630	top:
631	  preconditions
632	
633	bottom:
634	  postconditions
635	
636	hidden/side/internal layers:
637	  error handling
638	  tracing
639	  logging
640	  assertions
641	  resource cleanup
642	  debugging hooks
643	  instrumentation
644	This means Tuplet3D can explore aspect weaving visually.
645	
646	Possible aspect categories:
647	
648	Aspect	Purpose
649	pre	Preconditions before invocation
650	post	Postconditions after invocation
651	around	Wrap call or control flow
652	on-error	Error handling
653	trace	Logging / execution trace
654	debug	Breakpoints / watchpoints
655	profile	Timing / counters
656	macro	Compile-time rewriting
657	emit	Target-code generation
658	test	Examples and assertions
659	A compiler or tool could “weave” aspects into the executable representation.
660	
661	6. Macro expansion and rear view
662	The rear view should represent what the tool derives from the source.
663	
664	Possible rear-view targets:
665	
666	macro-expanded Tuplet3D
667	flattened Tuplet
668	Forth
669	COR24 assembler
670	Lisp
671	p-code
672	bytecode
673	visual IR
674	The rear view should probably be tool-selectable.
675	
676	Example:
677	
678	rear target = Forth
679	rear target = COR24 asm
680	rear target = macro-expanded source
681	rear target = normalized core IR
682	So “rear view” means:
683	
684	what this definition becomes after transformation
685	not one fixed target language.
686	
687	7. Source library / dictionary view
688	Tuplet3D should support editing a prelude or dictionary of user-minted forms.
689	
690	A dictionary is a spatial collection of definitions.
691	
692	Rather than listing definitions alphabetically, the tool can arrange them in a 3D grid.
693	
694	Definitions may be grouped by:
695	
696	arity
697	input type
698	output type
699	stack effect
700	tuple shape
701	domain
702	category
703	implementation strategy
704	precondition complexity
705	postcondition style
706	error behavior
707	macro expansion target
708	pipeline role
709	Example library operations:
710	
711	rotate all definitions to left view
712	  → compare inputs
713	
714	rotate all definitions to right view
715	  → compare outputs
716	
717	rotate all definitions to top view
718	  → compare preconditions
719	
720	rotate all definitions to bottom view
721	  → compare postconditions
722	
723	rotate all definitions to rear view
724	  → inspect generated Forth/assembler
725	
726	sort definitions by arity on X axis
727	sort definitions by input type on Y axis
728	sort definitions by output type on Z axis
729	filter to numeric verbs only
730	highlight verbs that can compose with selected output type
731	This is a major use-case.
732	
733	8. Pipeline/network view
734	Tuplet3D is not only about definition source.
735	
736	Minted verbs can be connected into a spatial pipeline network.
737	
738	The network is 3D plus time:
739	
740	3D space:
741	  nodes = minted verbs
742	  edges = tuple/data/control flow
743	
744	4D with time:
745	  execution, animation, debugging, traces
746	Pipeline visualization should support:
747	
748	rotate
749	zoom
750	pan
751	filter
752	highlight
753	expand
754	collapse
755	step through execution
756	show tuple values moving through edges
757	show precondition checks
758	show postcondition checks
759	show macro-expanded or lowered nodes
760	show instrumentation overlays
761	show errors and failed contracts
762	This makes Tuplet3D both a language and a visual execution/debugging environment.
763	
764	9. Important use-cases
765	Use-case A: Author a single minted verb
766	User creates a definition with:
767	
768	front algorithm gist
769	
770	left input signature
771	
772	right output signature
773	
774	top preconditions
775	
776	bottom postconditions
777	
778	rear generated target view
779	
780	Goal:
781	
782	Create a small spatial definition that can compile to Forth/COR24.
783	Use-case B: Hide complexity while preserving correctness
784	The front view should stay readable.
785	
786	Details such as error handling, contracts, tracing, and expansion should not clutter the main algorithm view.
787	
788	Goal:
789	
790	Make small algorithms explainable without deleting engineering detail.
791	Use-case C: Inspect generated code
792	User rotates to rear view or selects rear target:
793	
794	macro expansion
795	Forth
796	COR24 assembler
797	Lisp
798	IR
799	Goal:
800	
801	Understand what the tool generated and debug transformation errors.
802	Use-case D: Compare a library of minted verbs
803	User loads a dictionary/prelude and rotates all definitions to compare one aspect.
804	
805	Examples:
806	
807	show all input signatures
808	show all output signatures
809	show all preconditions
810	show all postconditions
811	show all generated Forth forms
812	Goal:
813	
814	Organize and understand the language dictionary visually.
815	Use-case E: Sort and group definitions spatially
816	User sorts definitions by:
817	
818	arity
819	input type
820	output type
821	tuple shape
822	effect category
823	domain
824	implementation kind
825	Goal:
826	
827	Discover related verbs and candidate compositions.
828	Use-case F: Compose verbs into a pipeline
829	User drags minted verbs into a 3D network.
830	
831	Connections are type/tuple-aware.
832	
833	Goal:
834	
835	Build programs from spatially composed minted verbs.
836	Use-case G: Debug pipeline execution

file.content:
I want to take Tuplet, my COR24-based experimental language with first-class tuples, user "minting" verbs for most of the language, currently parsed in OCaml and compiled to Forth and derive a new language from that.  This new language builds on the 2D whitespace parsing nature of Tuple where the mint character does not appear until line 3 or later, but extends this into 3D space.  So now the glyphs can be viewed from 6 directions (front, left side, right side, rear, top, and bottom).  Every character (or symbol which may be a word of symbols) resides at a 3D coordinate.  A definition (one minting) can be viewed from the side to see the essence (while hiding details in different Z planes from the user's perspective.

I’ll treat this as a language-design derivation problem: preserve Tuplet’s “minted verbs + spatial syntax” idea, then lift the source model from 2D sheets to a 3D artifact with multiple projections and hidden/detail planes.

Thought for 4s
Restated direction
You want a Tuplet-derived language where source code is no longer just a 2D glyph layout. It becomes a 3D source object:

Each glyph/token/word has an (x, y, z) coordinate.

The same definition can be viewed from six orthographic directions:

front

rear

left

right

top

bottom

A “minting” definition may reveal different structure depending on view.

Side views can expose the essence of a definition while hiding detail layers.

Tuplet’s “mint character appears only on line 3 or later” idea generalizes into depth-delayed structure.

A good working name might be:

Prism
Because the language is not read once; it is projected.

Other names:

Name	Emphasis
Prism	one object, many views
Glyphspace	spatial glyph language
Mintspace	Tuplet heritage
Voxel	3D source-code cells
Lattice	coordinates, planes, relations
Facet	definitions viewed from multiple faces
Coreform	side view reveals essence
I’d probably use Prism as the derived language name and keep Tuplet as the 2D ancestor.

Core idea
A source file is not text. It is a voxel document.

Source = set of positioned symbols

Symbol {
    id
    text
    x
    y
    z
    metadata
}
A “program” is parsed by projecting the 3D source object onto one or more 2D planes.

front view  = project along -Z
rear view   = project along +Z
left view   = project along +X
right view  = project along -X
top view    = project along -Y
bottom view = project along +Y
So the same definition has multiple legal readings.

Important design choice
You probably do not want all six views to be independent programs.

That would be too magical.

Instead:

Recommended rule
Each definition has one primary view and several auxiliary views.

For example:

View	Meaning
front	readable declaration / API shape
side	essence / algorithm summary
top	type/data-flow relationships
rear	lowered/internal expansion
bottom	tests/examples/proofs
hidden Z planes	implementation details
This gives the language a clear architecture:

definition = visible surface + concealed implementation layers
That maps nicely to your goal: the side view shows the “essence” while details live in other depth planes.

Minting in 3D
Tuplet has a delayed mint character in 2D. Prism could generalize that into a mint depth rule.

In 2D Tuplet:

line 1: visible phrase
line 2: maybe type/shape
line 3+: mint appears
In 3D Prism:

surface plane: visible signature
inner plane 1: constraints / tuple shape
inner plane 2+: minting glyph appears
deeper planes: implementation
So a definition might be physically structured like this:

z = 0   public phrase / name / tuple shape
z = 1   types, guards, preconditions
z = 2   minting verb appears
z = 3+  implementation
That is a strong, simple rule.

Example model
Imagine a Power definition.

Front view:

Power (n : ℤ  e : ℤ) → (p : ℤ)
Side view:

1 →
loop e times
n × →
Top view:

n ─────┐
       × repeated e times ── p
e ─────┘
Rear view:

: POWER
  ... generated Forth ...
;
Bottom view:

Power(2, 8) → 256
Power(5, 0) → 1
Power(3, 3) → 27
The same definition is a small cube of meaning, not a flat text block.

Parsing architecture
I would keep your current OCaml parser, but add a stage before it.

Current Tuplet pipeline
Tuplet text
  → OCaml parser
  → AST
  → Forth
  → COR24 path
Prism pipeline
Prism 3D source
  → voxel loader
  → projection engine
  → view parsers
  → merged semantic graph
  → core IR
  → Forth
  → COR24
The key addition is the semantic graph.

Do not immediately flatten six views into text. Instead, collect facts:

symbol A is at (x,y,z)
symbol A projects near symbol B in front view
symbol C is hidden behind symbol D
mint glyph appears at depth z=2
side projection gives essence form
rear projection gives lowering hint
Then build a definition from those facts.

Source representation
For an MVP, do not invent a full 3D editor first.

Use a plain text carrier format.

Example:

@z 0
Power (n : ℤ  e : ℤ) → (p : ℤ)

@z 1
requires e >= 0

@z 2
        ← mint

@z 3
p := 1
loop e times:
    p := p × n
Or more explicitly:

glyph "Power" at 0 0 0
glyph "("     at 6 0 0
glyph "n"     at 7 0 0
glyph ":"     at 9 0 0
glyph "ℤ"     at 11 0 0
...
glyph "←"     at 12 2 2
For human editing, layered text is easier. For machine testing, coordinate form is easier.

I’d support both:

.prism     human layered source
.prism.json canonical coordinate source
Minimal MVP semantics
Do not start with fully general 3D language semantics.

Start with one useful case:

MVP: 3D definitions with projection metadata
A definition consists of:

front plane: public definition surface

side plane: essence

depth planes: implementation

rear plane: optional generated/lowered code

bottom plane: examples/tests

Then define one compiler rule:

front + implementation → executable definition
side/top/bottom → checked annotations
That keeps the language executable while letting the other views become meaningful.

Low-level representation
In OCaml:

type coord = {
  x : int;
  y : int;
  z : int;
}

type glyph = {
  id : int;
  text : string;
  coord : coord;
}

type direction =
  | Front
  | Rear
  | Left
  | Right
  | Top
  | Bottom

type projection_token = {
  glyph_id : int;
  text : string;
  u : int;
  v : int;
  depth : int;
  occluded : bool;
}

type definition = {
  name : string;
  glyphs : glyph list;
  primary_view : direction;
}
Projection is just coordinate transformation.

For example:

let project_front g =
  { u = g.coord.x; v = g.coord.y; depth = g.coord.z }
let project_left g =
  { u = g.coord.z; v = g.coord.y; depth = g.coord.x }
Then your existing 2D Tuplet parser can parse a projection.

That is the big win:

3D Prism source
  → projection
  → existing Tuplet-ish 2D parser
Occlusion rules
You need rules for what happens when two glyphs occupy the same projected coordinate.

Options:

Option A: nearest wins
From a given view, only the nearest glyph is visible.

Pros:

intuitive

matches physical 3D viewing

supports hidden implementation layers

Cons:

hidden glyphs may be hard to discover

accidental occlusion can cause confusing errors

Option B: stack shown as overlay
Projected collisions become a stack.

Pros:

better for debugging

no accidental data loss

Cons:

less visually elegant

Option C: nearest wins for normal parsing, stack available to tooling
This is probably best.

compiler view: nearest visible glyph
editor/debug view: full depth stack
Language meaning of “essence”
The side view should probably not be executable code at first.

It should be a compressed semantic annotation.

For example, side view might declare:

identity
loop
fold
map
guard
dispatch
recurse
emit
So side view answers:

What kind of definition is this?

Examples:

Side-view essence	Meaning
map	transforms each element
fold	accumulates
guard	validates/preconditions
dispatch	chooses among verbs
loop	iteration
recurse	recursive definition
literal	constant/function table
ffi	external primitive
This gives you a way to compare the side view with the implementation.

If the side view says fold, but the implementation is recursive branching, the compiler can warn.

Strong design principle
The six views should be consistent, not independent.

A definition is valid only if its projections agree.

Example consistency checks:

front view says result tuple is (p : ℤ)
implementation must produce p

side view says loop
implementation must contain loop/fold/repetition

bottom view examples must typecheck and pass

top view dataflow must mention all inputs and outputs
This turns 3D syntax from a gimmick into a useful correctness tool.

Compilation target
Since Tuplet currently compiles to Forth, Prism should probably compile to the same intermediate target initially.

Prism
  → projected Tuplet forms
  → Tuplet AST
  → Prism semantic graph
  → normalized Tuplet/Core IR
  → Forth
  → COR24
Avoid compiling six views independently.

Instead compile one canonical core.

Suggested first demo
Implement only this:

Power definition as a 3D source cube
Views:

View	Content
front	function signature
left	essence: identity / loop / multiply
top	dataflow from n,e to p
rear	generated Forth
bottom	examples
depth layers	actual implementation
Then build a small web UI:

[front] [left] [right] [rear] [top] [bottom]
Plus a slider:

z-plane: 0 1 2 3 4
This would make the idea immediately understandable.

Risks
Risk 1: too much visual cleverness
A 3D language can become unreadable fast.

Mitigation:

make front view sufficient for normal reading

make side/top/bottom views optional but checkable

use depth for controlled hiding, not arbitrary art

Risk 2: editor burden
Nobody wants to hand-edit 3D coordinates.

Mitigation:

layered textual format first

Yew/WASM visualizer later

coordinate JSON only as canonical IR

Risk 3: ambiguous projection semantics
Multiple glyphs can collide in projection.

Mitigation:

deterministic nearest-visible rule

tooling shows hidden stacks

compiler warns on accidental occlusion

Risk 4: six views imply six languages
Mitigation:

one executable language

other views are annotations, contracts, examples, or lowerings

Best architecture
I’d structure the implementation as:

prism-source
  parse layered text into glyph coordinates

prism-geom
  projection, rotation, visibility, occlusion

prism-view
  convert projected glyphs into 2D token grids

tuplet-parser
  existing / adapted parser

prism-sema
  merge views into semantic graph

prism-core
  normalized executable IR

prism-forth
  compile to Forth

prism-ui
  optional Yew/WASM visualizer
The key insight
Tuplet is a 2D language where layout has meaning.

Prism would be a 3D language where projection has meaning.

That gives you a powerful metaphor:

front view: what it is
side view: what it means
top view: how data flows
inside: how it works
rear view: what it becomes
bottom view: how we know
That is a very clean conceptual model.


slow down.  You have presented some good ideas, but I have not finished capturing my vision of this language and the 3D tools around it.  I wanted to consider some ideas like aspect oriented programming, pre/post conditions, macro expansion, instrumentation, debugging, etc.  So my initial idea was front view gets the gist of an algorithm across, hiding pre/post conditions, error handling.  The left view shows inputs, type, signature.  The right side shows outputs, type, signature.  The top view shows pre-conditions.  Bottom view shows post-confitions.  Rear view shows macro expansion or "binary" (assembler, Forth, lisp--whatever the  tool transpiles Tuplet3D source into (better name later).  

While I mentioned the 6 sides, we are not limited to 6 views.  We can fold a higer dimension aspect-mapping into a 6-sided projection of n>6 sides.  Similar to PCA in ML we could show the most relevant 6 sides in 3D (and one side in 2D) or a set of projections as multiple side-by-side 2D views of fewer than 6 sides or more than 6 sides.

Imagine plumbing these together in 3D (the minted verb, not the definition) into a 3D (4D counting time) pipeline network whic could be visualized, rotated, zoomed, filtered, highlighted, expanded, etc.

Back to the source view, imagine editing a prelude or dictionary of user-minted forms.  We could view a 3D grid of 3D definitions.  We can group related definitions across 2D planes or slices of the 3D library grid. We could rotate all definitions to show all their inputs (arity, types) at once and sort that view to group by input arrity on one dimiension and types on another.  We could rotate all to the other 6 "standard" views to compare (or observe) pre/post conditions, error handling, implementation details, etc.  

Need a detailed set of use-cases, specifications, and enough initial research that an AI Coding agent could derive docs/{architecture,PRD,design,plan}.md files from your detailed description.

Understood — I’ll treat the current language as still being captured, not finalized. I’ll expand your vision into a structured requirements/use-case/specification packet that a coding agent can turn into docs/{architecture,PRD,design,plan}.md, while preserving open design space instead of prematurely narrowing it.

Thought for a few seconds
Below is a source packet for an AI coding agent. It is intentionally written as detailed input material, not as final architecture.

Tuplet3D Vision Capture Packet
1. Working concept
Tuplet3D is a derived language and tool environment based on Tuplet.

Tuplet currently explores:

COR24-oriented experimental language design.

First-class named tuples.

User-minted verbs.

2D whitespace/spatial parsing.

OCaml parsing.

Compilation/transpilation to Forth.

Tuplet3D extends this idea into a spatial programming environment where source forms, definitions, aspects, pipelines, generated code, debugging views, and semantic projections can all be represented in 3D or higher-dimensional abstract space.

The key distinction:

Tuplet:
  Meaning emerges from 2D textual/spatial layout.

Tuplet3D:
  Meaning emerges from 3D/higher-dimensional projected forms,
  where each view reveals a semantic aspect of the same minted verb.
2. Core source model
A Tuplet3D source object is not just a text file.

It is a collection of positioned symbols:

symbol {
  text: glyph | word | operator | minted verb | annotation
  coordinate: x, y, z
  optional_aspect_coordinate: a0, a1, a2, ...
  metadata
}
A definition is a bounded spatial object.

A minted verb is the callable/semantic entity created by a definition.

The definition may be viewed from multiple directions or projected through different aspect mappings.

3. Standard semantic views
The first standard six views are:

View	Meaning
Front	Algorithm gist / essential readable story
Left	Inputs, arity, parameter names, input types
Right	Outputs, result names, output types
Top	Preconditions
Bottom	Postconditions
Rear	Expansion / generated form / transpiled output
This is better than treating views as arbitrary faces. Each face has a job.

Example
For a Power minted verb:

View	Example content
Front	“start with 1; multiply by n, e times”
Left	n : ℤ, e : ℤ
Right	p : ℤ
Top	e ≥ 0
Bottom	p = n^e
Rear	Forth, assembler, Lisp, or macro-expanded Tuplet
4. Not limited to six views
Tuplet3D should not assume only six semantic aspects.

The six physical faces are only a projection device.

There may be many aspects:

inputs
outputs
types
preconditions
postconditions
invariants
error handling
macro expansion
instrumentation
debug hooks
tests
examples
contracts
cost model
memory behavior
stack effects
dataflow
control flow
generated Forth
generated COR24 assembler
generated Lisp
documentation
visual explanation
These can be mapped into six currently visible faces.

This is analogous to choosing a projection of higher-dimensional data.

The UI should eventually support:

many semantic aspects
  → select/relevance-rank/project
  → show as six 3D faces
  → or show as multiple 2D panels
5. Aspect-oriented programming connection
Tuplet3D has a natural relationship to aspect-oriented programming.

A definition’s core algorithm may be visible in the front view, while other aspects are spatially attached but visually separable:

front:
  algorithm gist

top:
  preconditions

bottom:
  postconditions

hidden/side/internal layers:
  error handling
  tracing
  logging
  assertions
  resource cleanup
  debugging hooks
  instrumentation
This means Tuplet3D can explore aspect weaving visually.

Possible aspect categories:

Aspect	Purpose
pre	Preconditions before invocation
post	Postconditions after invocation
around	Wrap call or control flow
on-error	Error handling
trace	Logging / execution trace
debug	Breakpoints / watchpoints
profile	Timing / counters
macro	Compile-time rewriting
emit	Target-code generation
test	Examples and assertions
A compiler or tool could “weave” aspects into the executable representation.

6. Macro expansion and rear view
The rear view should represent what the tool derives from the source.

Possible rear-view targets:

macro-expanded Tuplet3D
flattened Tuplet
Forth
COR24 assembler
Lisp
p-code
bytecode
visual IR
The rear view should probably be tool-selectable.

Example:

rear target = Forth
rear target = COR24 asm
rear target = macro-expanded source
rear target = normalized core IR
So “rear view” means:

what this definition becomes after transformation
not one fixed target language.

7. Source library / dictionary view
Tuplet3D should support editing a prelude or dictionary of user-minted forms.

A dictionary is a spatial collection of definitions.

Rather than listing definitions alphabetically, the tool can arrange them in a 3D grid.

Definitions may be grouped by:

arity
input type
output type
stack effect
tuple shape
domain
category
implementation strategy
precondition complexity
postcondition style
error behavior
macro expansion target
pipeline role
Example library operations:

rotate all definitions to left view
  → compare inputs

rotate all definitions to right view
  → compare outputs

rotate all definitions to top view
  → compare preconditions

rotate all definitions to bottom view
  → compare postconditions

rotate all definitions to rear view
  → inspect generated Forth/assembler

sort definitions by arity on X axis
sort definitions by input type on Y axis
sort definitions by output type on Z axis
filter to numeric verbs only
highlight verbs that can compose with selected output type
This is a major use-case.

8. Pipeline/network view
Tuplet3D is not only about definition source.

Minted verbs can be connected into a spatial pipeline network.

The network is 3D plus time:

3D space:
  nodes = minted verbs
  edges = tuple/data/control flow

4D with time:
  execution, animation, debugging, traces
Pipeline visualization should support:

rotate
zoom
pan
filter
highlight
expand
collapse
step through execution
show tuple values moving through edges
show precondition checks
show postcondition checks
show macro-expanded or lowered nodes
show instrumentation overlays
show errors and failed contracts
This makes Tuplet3D both a language and a visual execution/debugging environment.

9. Important use-cases
Use-case A: Author a single minted verb
User creates a definition with:

front algorithm gist

left input signature

right output signature

top preconditions

bottom postconditions

rear generated target view

Goal:

Create a small spatial definition that can compile to Forth/COR24.
Use-case B: Hide complexity while preserving correctness
The front view should stay readable.

Details such as error handling, contracts, tracing, and expansion should not clutter the main algorithm view.

Goal:

Make small algorithms explainable without deleting engineering detail.
Use-case C: Inspect generated code
User rotates to rear view or selects rear target:

macro expansion
Forth
COR24 assembler
Lisp
IR
Goal:

Understand what the tool generated and debug transformation errors.
Use-case D: Compare a library of minted verbs
User loads a dictionary/prelude and rotates all definitions to compare one aspect.

Examples:

show all input signatures
show all output signatures
show all preconditions
show all postconditions
show all generated Forth forms
Goal:

Organize and understand the language dictionary visually.
Use-case E: Sort and group definitions spatially
User sorts definitions by:

arity
input type
output type
tuple shape
effect category
domain
implementation kind
Goal:

Discover related verbs and candidate compositions.
Use-case F: Compose verbs into a pipeline
User drags minted verbs into a 3D network.

Connections are type/tuple-aware.

Goal:

Build programs from spatially composed minted verbs.
Use-case G: Debug pipeline execution
User runs the pipeline and sees:

which node fired
which tuple moved
which precondition passed/failed
which postcondition passed/failed
which generated code executed
where instrumentation was inserted
Goal:

Make execution observable and explainable.
Use-case H: Aspect weaving
User enables/disables aspects:

trace
profile
assert
debug
error recovery
logging
Goal:

See how aspects modify execution without polluting the front algorithm view.
Use-case I: Educational explanation
A teacher or video creator rotates a definition:

front: algorithm story
left: inputs
right: outputs
top: assumptions
bottom: guarantees
rear: machine form
Goal:

Explain programming language concepts visually.
10. Initial research analogs
The coding agent should research or compare against these ideas:

Area	Relevant analogy
Aspect-oriented programming	cross-cutting concerns, weaving
Design by contract	preconditions, postconditions, invariants
Literate programming	explanatory source views
Visual programming	node graphs, dataflow editors
Smalltalk browsers	class/method/system browsing
Forth dictionaries	minted words and ordered vocabularies
APL/J/K	dense symbolic expression
Projectional editing	source as structured object, not plain text
Hypertext systems	multiple linked views of the same object
CAD / 3D modeling	rotate, slice, hide, expose internal structure
PCA / dimensionality reduction	map many semantic dimensions into selected views
Debug visualizers	traces, watchpoints, value flow
Macro systems	source-to-source expansion
Compiler IR viewers	AST, lowered IR, assembly views
Dataflow languages	pipelines, nodes, edges
Functional composition	typed function composition
Stack languages	stack effects, Forth lowering
11. Suggested docs the agent should produce
The coding agent should generate:

docs/architecture.md
docs/PRD.md
docs/design.md
docs/plan.md
architecture.md
Should cover:

Tuplet3D conceptual model.

Source object model.

Coordinates and projections.

Definition object.

Aspect model.

Dictionary/prelude model.

Pipeline/network model.

Compiler/transpiler pipeline.

UI architecture.

Relationship to existing Tuplet OCaml parser and Forth backend.

PRD.md
Should cover:

Product goals.

User personas.

Use-cases.

Non-goals.

MVP scope.

Future scope.

UX principles.

Success criteria.

Risks.

design.md
Should cover:

Data structures.

File formats.

Projection rules.

Standard semantic views.

Aspect mapping model.

Library grid model.

Pipeline graph model.

Compiler pipeline.

Debug/instrumentation model.

Example definitions.

plan.md
Should cover:

Milestones.

MVP implementation phases.

Test strategy.

Demo strategy.

Risks and mitigations.

Suggested first prototype.

12. Recommended MVP
The first prototype should avoid a full 3D editor.

Build:

Tuplet3D textual layered source
  → coordinate model
  → six projections
  → visual renderer
  → simple parser hooks
  → Forth output placeholder or real backend reuse
MVP features:

Load one definition.

Show six standard views.

Rotate between views.

Show hidden/detail layers.

Parse left/right/top/bottom/front/rear as separate aspect planes.

Generate a normalized internal object.

Emit a placeholder Forth form.

Display dictionary grid with several example verbs.

Allow sorting by input arity and output type.

Show a tiny pipeline of 3 minted verbs.

13. First demo definitions
Use small definitions:

Identity
Add
Power
Clamp
Abs
Map
Fold
Filter
Each should have:

front gist
left inputs
right outputs
top preconditions
bottom postconditions
rear generated Forth-ish output
14. Key design principle
Tuplet3D should not be “3D syntax for its own sake.”

The core value is:

separate semantic aspects spatially,
then let the user rotate, project, sort, compose, inspect, and debug them.
The language should make programs more explainable, not merely more decorative.

content.content:
# tuplet

An experimental infix programming language with first-class
named tuple bundles, multi-output verbs, and call-site
argument splicing -- compiled to Forth and executed on the
COR24 runtime.

![tuplet logo](images/tuplet-logo.jpg)

## What's special

Tuplet's distinguishing feature is that **almost everything
is user-extensible**. The kernel is ~10 forms; everything
else (`if/then/else`, `while`, every operator, every helper)
lives in `lib/std.tup` as a `*syntax` declaration that
ordinary Tuplet users can read, replace, or write more of.

The single mechanism is **mint** (`*`):

```
*syntax do _ while _ end expand
  prim/forth "BEGIN"  _1  _2  prim/forth "0= UNTIL"

*n <- 0
do
  n <- n + 1
  n prim/forth "."
  prim/forth "SPACE"
while  n < 3  end
# 1 2 3
```

The user writes that, the language extends. No compiler
change.

## Status

Phase 0 (scaffold) and the early steps of Phase 1 (lexer)
are complete. The interpreter, IR, Forth emitter, prelude,
and demo gallery are upcoming sagas. See `docs/plan.md` for
the arc and `docs/poc-goals.md` for the demo target.

## Documentation

- [`docs/poc-goals.md`](docs/poc-goals.md) -- the single
  demoable target.
- [`docs/prd.md`](docs/prd.md) -- product requirements.
- [`docs/grammar.md`](docs/grammar.md) -- surface syntax.
- [`docs/kernel.md`](docs/kernel.md) -- the kernel/prelude
  boundary.
- [`docs/design.md`](docs/design.md) -- AST and stack IR.
- [`docs/lowering.md`](docs/lowering.md) -- IR -> Forth.
- [`docs/lexer.md`](docs/lexer.md) -- lexer host notes.
- [`docs/plan.md`](docs/plan.md) -- saga arc.
- [`docs/dsl-examples.md`](docs/dsl-examples.md) -- DSL
  extensions a user might write.
- [`docs/language-wishlist.md`](docs/language-wishlist.md) --
  layered preludes, error handling, modules; design research
  for what comes after the PoC.
- [`docs/tooling-smoke.md`](docs/tooling-smoke.md) -- how to
  rerun the wiring-confidence tests.
- [`docs/glyphs.md`](docs/glyphs.md) -- the alphabet of
  Tuplet glyphs (minimal + suggested).
- [`docs/emacs-inputs.md`](docs/emacs-inputs.md) -- entering
  Tuplet glyphs in Emacs (TeX, Agda, custom Quail).
- [`docs/cli-inputs.md`](docs/cli-inputs.md) -- entering
  glyphs anywhere via Espanso, XCompose, AHK, etc.
- [`demos/`](demos/) -- aspirational `.tup` examples.

## Project layout

```
src/                Tuplet host code (OCaml subset on sw-cor24-ocaml)
tests/              Test fixtures + reg-rs inputs
work/reg-rs/        Golden baselines (.rgt + .out tracked)
docs/               Specs, design, wishlist
demos/              Aspirational .tup programs
scripts/            Build/run helpers
images/             Logo
.agentrail/         Saga / step durable record

content.content:
1	# Tuplet -- Surface Grammar
2	
3	This document specifies the Tuplet surface syntax. ASCII is the
4	primary form; a small Unicode alias table is given for authoring in
5	the research notation.
6	
7	## File extension
8	
9	`.tup`
10	
11	## Lexical rules
12	
13	- **Whitespace.** Spaces and tabs separate tokens. Line breaks
14	  terminate statements (see Grammar).
15	- **Comments.** `#` starts a line comment; the rest of the line is
16	  ignored.
17	- **Identifiers (names).** Start with an ASCII letter, followed by
18	  any combination of letters, digits, or `_`. May end with `?`.
19	  Trailing digits are significant: they declare arity for tuple-
20	  producing names (see Arity rules). Examples: `coord2`, `point4`,
21	  `x`, `success?`, `Red`.
22	- **Integer literals.** Optional leading `-`, one or more digits.
23	  Examples: `0`, `42`, `-7`.
24	- **Percent literals.** An integer literal immediately followed by
25	  `%`. Examples: `50%`, `100%`.
26	- **Punctuation.** `,` `(` `)`
27	- **Mint operator.** Canonical Unicode: `BULLET` (U+2022).
28	  Aliases also recognized by the lexer: `BLACK SMALL
29	  SQUARE` (U+25AA). ASCII fallback **proposed `*`**
30	  (small filled mark; needs user confirmation -- alternatives:
31	  `!`, `mint `, `def `). The mint operator must precede every
32	  NEW name binding: tuple-var declaration, verb signature,
33	  user-defined verb body, `syntax` declaration. Example:
34	  `*coord2 -> (x y)` mints the tuple variable; subsequent
35	  `coord2 <- 3, 9` is an ordinary assignment. Without the mint
36	  operator, references to unbound names are errors. This is
37	  the construct the DSL user reaches for whenever they want
38	  to extend the language.
39	- **Assignment.** `<-`
40	- **Mapping (signature arrow).** `->`
41	- **Binary operators.** Symbolic: `+`, `-`, `*` (caveat: `*` may
42	  alias the mint operator depending on the chosen ASCII fallback;
43	  context-sensitive lexing or a different fallback resolves it).
44	  Named: `max`, `min`, `div`, `max2`, `min2`, `div2`. Named
45	  operators follow identifier rules lexically.
46	
47	## Unicode aliases
48	
49	Accepted by the lexer as exact synonyms for their ASCII forms:
50	
51	| ASCII  | Unicode |
52	|--------|---------|
53	| `*` (TBD) | mint operator: BULLET (U+2022) -- also accepts BLACK SMALL SQUARE (U+25AA) |
54	| `<-`   | LEFTWARDS ARROW         (U+27F5 or U+2190) |
55	| `->`   | heavy mapping arrow     (U+2500 x3 + U+2023) |
56	| `(`    | shell-bracket left      (U+239B or U+2983) |
57	| `)`    | shell-bracket right     (U+239E or U+2984) |
58	| `{`    | LEFT CURLY BRACKET UPPER HOOK  (U+23A7) -- block start (TBD) |
59	| `}`    | RIGHT CURLY BRACKET UPPER HOOK (U+23AB) -- block end (TBD)   |
60	| `max`  | wedge up                (U+22CF) |
61	| `min`  | wedge down              (U+22CE) |
62	| `max2` | wedge up + subscript 2  (U+22CF U+2082) |
63	| `min2` | wedge down + subscript 2 (U+22CE U+2082) |
64	| `div`  | division sign           (U+00F7) |
65	| `div2` | division sign + sub 2   (U+00F7 U+2082) |
66	| `?`    | superscript question    (U+02C0) |
67	| `2`    | subscript 2             (U+2082) -- arity suffix |
68	
69	The lexer must fold Unicode aliases to their ASCII spelling before
70	the parser sees them. ASCII stays canonical in diagnostics.
71	
72	## Grammar (EBNF)
73	
74	```
75	program      ::= { statement NEWLINE }
76	statement    ::= comment
77	               | declaration
78	               | signature
79	               | assignment
80	               | expression
81	
82	comment      ::= "#" { any-char }
83	
84	declaration  ::= mint name "->" "(" field-list ")" [ "<-" expr-list ]
85	field-list   ::= name { name }
86	
87	signature    ::= mint name "(" [ field-list ] ")" "->" "(" field-list ")" [ "<-" expr ]
88	mint         ::= "*" | "U+2022" | "U+25AA"   (* mint operator *)
89	
90	assignment   ::= lvalue "<-" expr
91	lvalue       ::= name { "," name }
92	
93	expression   ::= expr
94	expr         ::= primary
95	               | expr bin-op expr
96	               | call
97	
98	call         ::= name "(" { arg } ")"
99	arg          ::= primary
100	
101	expr-list    ::= expr { "," expr }
102	
103	primary      ::= int-lit
104	               | pct-lit
105	               | name
106	               | "(" expr ")"
107	
108	bin-op       ::= "+" | "-" | "*"
109	               | "max" | "min" | "div"
110	               | "max2" | "min2" | "div2"
111	```
112	
113	### Statement separation
114	
115	One statement per line. Blank lines are ignored. A statement may
116	not span multiple lines in the PoC; this simplifies the lexer.
117	
118	### Precedence and associativity
119	
120	For the PoC, all binary operators share one precedence level and
121	are left-associative. Use parentheses to disambiguate. A typing /
122	precedence pass is deferred.
123	
124	## Arity rules
125	
126	- Every expression has a static output arity (an integer >= 0).
127	- Integer and percent literals have arity 1.
128	- A bare name referring to a scalar has arity 1.
129	- A name whose last character run is digits declares a tuple-var
130	  of that arity (`coord2` -> 2, `point4` -> 4).
131	- `maxN`, `minN`, `divN` are multi-output operators of arity N.
132	  `max`, `min`, `div` with no suffix are arity 1.
133	- In a binary expression `a OP b`, the expression's arity is the
134	  operator's output arity.
135	- In a call `f(args)`, the call's arity is `f`'s declared output
136	  arity. The sum of each arg's arity (with splicing) must equal
137	  `f`'s declared input arity.
138	- In an assignment `lvalue <- expr`, the number of names on the
139	  LHS must equal the output arity of the RHS.
140	- In a declaration `name -> (f1 f2 ... fN)`, the name's arity is
141	  N. The declared field names are documentation; they do not
142	  introduce new bindings outside the declaration.
143	
144	## Splicing
145	
146	**Default policy.** In call position, every argument contributes
147	its full arity to the callee's input list. Tuple variables always
148	splice.
149	
150	Example:
151	
152	```
153	success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
154	# coord2 -> 2 values, Red -> 1, 50% -> 1; plot expects 4.
155	```
156	
157	**Open question (TODO).** There is no current syntax to pass a
158	tuple as a single boxed value. Candidates discussed in
159	`docs/research.txt`:
160	
161	- an explicit box marker, e.g. `plot(box(coord2) Red 50%)`,
162	- a no-splice unary operator, e.g. `plot(@coord2 Red 50%)`.
163	
164	Defer to a later spec saga. No splicing control is implemented
165	in the PoC.
166	
167	## Valid programs
168	
169	```
170	# 1a. Tuple var: mint and initialize separately.
171	*coord2 -> (x y)        # mint the tuple variable
172	coord2 <- 3, 9          # ordinary assignment to existing
173	a, b <- coord2          # destructure
174	```
175	
176	```
177	# 1b. Tuple var: mint and initialize in one statement.
178	*coord2 -> (x y) <- 3, 9
179	```
180	```
181	
182	```
183	# 2. Multi-output operator.
184	q, r <- 3 max2 5        # q=5, r=3
185	```
186	
187	```
188	# 3. Integer + fractional parts.
189	integer, fractional <- 7 div2 3
190	```
191	
192	```
193	# 4. Call-site splice.
194	*plot(x y color transparency) -> (success?)   # mint verb signature
195	*coord2 -> (x y)                               # mint tuple var
196	coord2 <- 3, 9
197	success? <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
198	```
199	
200	```
201	# 5. User-defined verb (mint with body).
202	*add(a b) -> (sum) <- a + b
203	n <- add(2 3)            # n = 5
204	```
205	
206	```
207	# 6. Mint a syntax extension (the heart of the PoC).
208	*syntax do _ while _ end expand
209	  prim/forth "BEGIN" _1 _2 prim/forth "UNTIL"
210	```
211	
212	## Invalid programs
213	
214	```
215	# 1. Arity mismatch on assignment LHS.
216	q <- a max2 b           # RHS arity 2, LHS arity 1
217	```
218	
219	```
220	# 2. Too few arguments to call after splicing.
221	plot(coord2 Red)        # arity 2 + 1 = 3, plot needs 4
222	```
223	
224	```
225	# 3. Tuple var mint with wrong field count for name arity.
226	*coord2 -> (x y z)      # name declares arity 2, fields list has 3
227	```
228	
229	```
230	# 5. Missing mint operator on first declaration.
231	coord2 -> (x y)         # `coord2` not yet bound; mint required
232	```
233	
234	```
235	# 4. Line-spanning statement.
236	coord2 <- 3,
237	         9              # statements don't span lines in the PoC
238	```
239

# Tuplet -- Surface Grammar

This document specifies the Tuplet surface syntax. ASCII is the
primary form; a small Unicode alias table is given for authoring in
the research notation.

## File extension

`.tup`

## Lexical rules

- **Whitespace.** Spaces and tabs separate tokens. Line breaks
  terminate statements (see Grammar).
- **Comments.** `#` starts a line comment; the rest of the line is
  ignored.
- **Identifiers (names).** Start with an ASCII letter, followed by
  any combination of letters, digits, or `_`. May end with `?`.
  Trailing digits are significant: they declare arity for tuple-
  producing names (see Arity rules). Examples: `coord2`, `point4`,
  `x`, `success?`, `Red`.
- **Integer literals.** Optional leading `-`, one or more digits.
  Examples: `0`, `42`, `-7`.
- **Percent literals.** An integer literal immediately followed by
  `%`. Examples: `50%`, `100%`.
- **Punctuation.** `,` `(` `)`
- **Mint operator.** Canonical Unicode: `BULLET` (U+2022).
  Aliases also recognized by the lexer: `BLACK SMALL
  SQUARE` (U+25AA). ASCII fallback **proposed `*`**
  (small filled mark; needs user confirmation -- alternatives:
  `!`, `mint `, `def `). The mint operator must precede every
  NEW name binding: tuple-var declaration, verb signature,
  user-defined verb body, `syntax` declaration. Example:
  `*coord2 -> (x y)` mints the tuple variable; subsequent
  `coord2 <- 3, 9` is an ordinary assignment. Without the mint
  operator, references to unbound names are errors. This is
  the construct the DSL user reaches for whenever they want
  to extend the language.
- **Assignment.** `<-`
- **Mapping (signature arrow).** `->`
- **Binary operators.** Symbolic: `+`, `-`, `*` (caveat: `*` may
  alias the mint operator depending on the chosen ASCII fallback;
  context-sensitive lexing or a different fallback resolves it).
  Named: `max`, `min`, `div`, `max2`, `min2`, `div2`. Named
  operators follow identifier rules lexically.

## Unicode aliases

Accepted by the lexer as exact synonyms for their ASCII forms:

| ASCII  | Unicode |
|--------|---------|
| `*` (TBD) | mint operator: BULLET (U+2022) -- also accepts BLACK SMALL SQUARE (U+25AA) |
| `<-`   | LEFTWARDS ARROW         (U+27F5 or U+2190) |
| `->`   | heavy mapping arrow     (U+2500 x3 + U+2023) |
| `(`    | shell-bracket left      (U+239B or U+2983) |
| `)`    | shell-bracket right     (U+239E or U+2984) |
| `{`    | LEFT CURLY BRACKET UPPER HOOK  (U+23A7) -- block start (TBD) |
| `}`    | RIGHT CURLY BRACKET UPPER HOOK (U+23AB) -- block end (TBD)   |
| `max`  | wedge up                (U+22CF) |
| `min`  | wedge down              (U+22CE) |
| `max2` | wedge up + subscript 2  (U+22CF U+2082) |
| `min2` | wedge down + subscript 2 (U+22CE U+2082) |
| `div`  | division sign           (U+00F7) |
| `div2` | division sign + sub 2   (U+00F7 U+2082) |
| `?`    | superscript question    (U+02C0) |
| `2`    | subscript 2             (U+2082) -- arity suffix |

The lexer must fold Unicode aliases to their ASCII spelling before
the parser sees them. ASCII stays canonical in diagnostics.

## Grammar (EBNF)

```
program      ::= { statement NEWLINE }
statement    ::= comment
               | declaration
               | signature
               | assignment
               | expression

comment      ::= "#" { any-char }

declaration  ::= mint name "->" "(" field-list ")" [ "<-" expr-list ]
field-list   ::= name { name }

signature    ::= mint name "(" [ field-list ] ")" "->" "(" field-list ")" [ "<-" expr ]
mint         ::= "*" | "U+2022" | "U+25AA"   (* mint operator *)

assignment   ::= lvalue "<-" expr
lvalue       ::= name { "," name }

expression   ::= expr
expr         ::= primary
               | expr bin-op expr
               | call

call         ::= name "(" { arg } ")"
arg          ::= primary

expr-list    ::= expr { "," expr }

primary      ::= int-lit
               | pct-lit
               | name
               | "(" expr ")"

bin-op       ::= "+" | "-" | "*"
               | "max" | "min" | "div"
               | "max2" | "min2" | "div2"
```

### Statement separation

One statement per line. Blank lines are ignored. A statement may
not span multiple lines in the PoC; this simplifies the lexer.

### Precedence and associativity

For the PoC, all binary operators share one precedence level and
are left-associative. Use parentheses to disambiguate. A typing /
precedence pass is deferred.

## Arity rules

- Every expression has a static output arity (an integer >= 0).
- Integer and percent literals have arity 1.
- A bare name referring to a scalar has arity 1.
- A name whose last character run is digits declares a tuple-var
  of that arity (`coord2` -> 2, `point4` -> 4).
- `maxN`, `minN`, `divN` are multi-output operators of arity N.
  `max`, `min`, `div` with no suffix are arity 1.
- In a binary expression `a OP b`, the expression's arity is the
  operator's output arity.
- In a call `f(args)`, the call's arity is `f`'s declared output
  arity. The sum of each arg's arity (with splicing) must equal
  `f`'s declared input arity.
- In an assignment `lvalue <- expr`, the number of names on the
  LHS must equal the output arity of the RHS.
- In a declaration `name -> (f1 f2 ... fN)`, the name's arity is
  N. The declared field names are documentation; they do not
  introduce new bindings outside the declaration.

## Splicing

**Default policy.** In call position, every argument contributes
its full arity to the callee's input list. Tuple variables always
splice.

Example:

```
success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
# coord2 -> 2 values, Red -> 1, 50% -> 1; plot expects 4.
```

**Open question (TODO).** There is no current syntax to pass a
tuple as a single boxed value. Candidates discussed in
`docs/research.txt`:

- an explicit box marker, e.g. `plot(box(coord2) Red 50%)`,
- a no-splice unary operator, e.g. `plot(@coord2 Red 50%)`.

Defer to a later spec saga. No splicing control is implemented
in the PoC.

## Valid programs

```
# 1a. Tuple var: mint and initialize separately.
*coord2 -> (x y)        # mint the tuple variable
coord2 <- 3, 9          # ordinary assignment to existing
a, b <- coord2          # destructure
```

```
# 1b. Tuple var: mint and initialize in one statement.
*coord2 -> (x y) <- 3, 9
```
```

```
# 2. Multi-output operator.
q, r <- 3 max2 5        # q=5, r=3
```

```
# 3. Integer + fractional parts.
integer, fractional <- 7 div2 3
```

```
# 4. Call-site splice.
*plot(x y color transparency) -> (success?)   # mint verb signature
*coord2 -> (x y)                               # mint tuple var
coord2 <- 3, 9
success? <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
```

```
# 5. User-defined verb (mint with body).
*add(a b) -> (sum) <- a + b
n <- add(2 3)            # n = 5
```

```
# 6. Mint a syntax extension (the heart of the PoC).
*syntax do _ while _ end expand
  prim/forth "BEGIN" _1 _2 prim/forth "UNTIL"
```

## Invalid programs

```
# 1. Arity mismatch on assignment LHS.
q <- a max2 b           # RHS arity 2, LHS arity 1
```

```
# 2. Too few arguments to call after splicing.
plot(coord2 Red)        # arity 2 + 1 = 3, plot needs 4
```

```
# 3. Tuple var mint with wrong field count for name arity.
*coord2 -> (x y z)      # name declares arity 2, fields list has 3
```

```
# 5. Missing mint operator on first declaration.
coord2 -> (x y)         # `coord2` not yet bound; mint required
```

```
# 4. Line-spanning statement.
coord2 <- 3,
         9              # statements don't span lines in the PoC
```

1	# Tuplet Lexer
2	
3	Implementation lives under `src/`; build/run via
4	`scripts/run-ml.sh <file.ml>` which delegates to
5	`sw-cor24-ocaml` and strips the Pascal runtime's source-echo
6	prefix so downstream output is the program's output only.
7	
8	## Smoke baseline
9	
10	`tuplet_build_skeleton` -- runs `src/lex_main.ml` and verifies
11	the cleaned output is `tuplet-lexer skeleton\n`.
12	
13	```
14	REG_RS_DATA_DIR=work/reg-rs reg-rs run -p tuplet_build_skeleton
15	```
16	
17	## Source-of-truth references
18	
19	- `docs/grammar.md` -- what the lexer must produce (token shape,
20	  Unicode aliases).
21	- `docs/kernel.md` -- the dynamic-literal-registry contract:
22	  `Lexer.add_literal` (or equivalent) lets the parser register
23	  new literal tokens at runtime as `syntax` declarations are
24	  processed.
25	
26	## Lexing model: byte stream
27	
28	The lexer is a byte-stream consumer. Input arrives via `getc`
29	(one byte at a time, 0..255); output token payload bytes are
30	emitted via `putc` (one byte at a time). This matches the
31	host's natural API and Forth's `KEY` / `EMIT` convention -- the
32	runtime is a byte-oriented system end-to-end.
33	
34	### EOF sentinel
35	
36	`getc` blocks at end of input rather than returning a sentinel.
37	The lexer uses `\x03` (ETX, ASCII 3) as the in-band EOF marker.
38	Every fixture file ends with one ETX byte; the lexer's main
39	loop returns when `getc` yields 3.
40	
41	The choice of ETX is arbitrary but stable: it is not a valid
42	character anywhere in Tuplet source and survives shell variable
43	round-tripping (which would strip `\x00`).
44	
45	### Driver
46	
47	`scripts/run-lexer-fixture.sh <fixture.input>` feeds a fixture
48	file's bytes via `OCAML_STDIN`, runs `src/lexer.ml`, strips the
49	Pascal runtime's source-echo prefixes, and prints just the
50	emitted tokens. reg-rs baselines drive this script per fixture.
51	
52	## Token representation
53	
54	The PoC uses idiomatic OCaml variants -- now supported upstream
55	since `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#2` was implemented (see commit
56	`17732be` on `feat/wip`):
57	
58	```ocaml
59	type token =
60	  | TIdent of string
61	  | TInt of int
62	  | TPct of int
63	  | TLArrow
64	  | TRArrow
65	  | TLParen
66	  | TRParen
67	  | TLBrace
68	  | TRBrace
69	  | TComma
70	  | TUnderscore
71	  | THash of string
72	  | TLiteral of string
73	  | TEOF
74	  | TMint
75	```
76	
77	Constructor payloads carry the relevant data; nullary
78	constructors have none. Dispatch via `match`.
79	
80	### Dump format
81	
82	`src/lexer.ml`'s `dump_tok` writes each token on its own line
83	via `putc` byte-by-byte: a fixed-width prefix for the kind
84	(stored as an `int list` of byte values, e.g. `[73; 78; 84;
85	32; 32; 32; 32]` is `"INT    "`), the payload bytes (for
86	`THash`) or the integer rendered as decimal digits (for
87	`TInt`/`TPct`), and a final `putc 10` for LF. Examples:
88	
89	```
90	HASH    hello world
91	INT    42
92	PCT    50
93	MINUS
94	UNK     <byte-int>
95	EOF
96	```
97	
98	### Numeric lexing
99	
100	Digits accumulate into the integer directly via `n * 10 + (b -
101	48)` while `is_digit b` holds. The terminating non-digit byte
102	is returned alongside the number; the main loop threads it
103	back as the next token's leading byte (one-byte lookahead via
104	a `pre` parameter, with `0` as the "no pending byte" sentinel
105	since `0x00` is not a valid Tuplet source byte). If the
106	post-digit byte is `%` (37) the token is `TPct`, otherwise
107	`TInt` and the lookahead byte starts the next token.
108	
109	## Multi-byte tokens (`<-`, `->`)
110	
111	Lookahead-driven. After reading `<` (60), peek the next byte;
112	if it's `-` (45) emit `TLArrow`. Otherwise `<` is unknown and
113	the lookahead byte is threaded back as the next token's
114	leading byte.
115	
116	`-` is now lookahead-driven too: read the next byte; if it's
117	`>` (62) emit `TRArrow`. Otherwise the token is `TMinus` and
118	the read byte is the next leading byte. The parser later
119	folds `TMinus` followed by `TInt` into a negative literal.
120	
121	## Single-byte punctuation
122	
123	| Byte | Char | Token        |
124	|------|------|--------------|
125	| 40   | `(`  | `TLParen`    |
126	| 41   | `)`  | `TRParen`    |
127	| 123  | `{`  | `TLBrace`    |
128	| 125  | `}`  | `TRBrace`    |
129	| 44   | `,`  | `TComma`     |
130	| 95   | `_`  | `TUnderscore`|
131	| 42   | `*`  | `TMint`      |
132	
133	## Source-ingestion vs runtime-input gotcha
134	
135	`getc` reads from the SAME UART stream that feeds source code
136	to the runtime. After source ingestion completes (EOT, 0x04),
137	runtime input begins. **The lexer driver `let _ = ... lex_loop
138	...` MUST be the last top-level statement** in the file: any
139	top-level statements after it would still be in the source
140	stream, and `getc` would consume their bytes instead of
141	runtime input. Discovered the hard way in step 005.
142	
143	## OCaml-subset notes (current)
144	
145	The `sw-cor24-ocaml` interpreter, since the recent fixes,
146	supports enough of OCaml to host nontrivial parser code
147	naturally. Confirmed:
148	
149	- **Top-level `let X = E` declarations** persist across lines
150	  (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#3`).
151	- **User-defined variant types** with payload constructors
152	  (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#2`).
153	- **Multi-file modules**: filename `math.ml` becomes module
154	  `Math`, accessed cross-file as `Math.add`. Multiple files
155	  passed as args: `run-ocaml.sh math.ml main.ml`. Top-level
156	  `let` in each file becomes a member of that module.
157	- **String escapes (`\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`)** and **3+ element
158	  tuples** (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#4`).
159	- Function-shorthand `let f x = ...` works.
160	- `let rec`, lists, pairs, options, pattern matching, qualified
161	  names (`List.length`, `List.rev`).
162	- `print_endline`, `print_int`, `string_of_int`, string concat
163	  via `^`, `int_of_string`.
164	- Byte I/O: `getc : unit -> int`, `putc : int -> unit`,
165	  `read_line : unit -> string`.
166	
167	The Tuplet lexer chooses the byte-stream `getc`/`putc` path
168	because it matches the host's natural API and the Forth runtime
169	target -- not because of a missing string-stdlib feature.
170	
171	## Open upstream limitations
172	
173	These are absent; the lexer's byte-stream design avoids needing
174	them, but they would simplify future work:
175	
176	- **`String.get` / `s.[i]` / `String.sub` / `Char.chr` /
177	  `Char.code` / `print_string`** -- per-character string access
178	  and char/int interop. The lexer side-steps the gap by reading
179	  bytes via `getc` and emitting via `putc`, never assembling
180	  strings from bytes nor pulling characters out of strings.
181	- **No string literals in match patterns.** `match x with "foo"
182	  -> ...` does not parse. With variants available, the lexer
183	  uses variant constructors for tag dispatch instead.
184	- **No `(* ... *)` block comments.** OCaml host code uses no
185	  comments for now.
186	
187	`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#4` (string `\n` escapes; 3+ element
188	tuples) was actually resolved by upstream fixes verified via
189	re-probing today; the issue may not yet be marked closed but
190	the runtime accepts both.
191	
192	## Implementation pattern (current)
193	
194	Each source file under `src/` is a self-contained program in
195	the natural OCaml style: top-level `type ...` declaration(s),
196	top-level `let ...` bindings, then `let _ = ...` to drive the
197	side-effects of the main flow. Multi-line, with each top-level
198	declaration on its own line. See `src/token_test.ml` for a
199	reference shape.
200	
201	Match arms must currently fit on one line (no multi-line
202	`match | ... | ...`); single-line dispatch via `match x with
203	PAT1 -> ... | PAT2 -> ...` is the idiom even when the arm
204	list is long.
205	
206	There is no module system / cross-file import. Each `.ml` file
207	is its own program. Code that several test files would share
208	is duplicated for now.
209

# Tuplet Lexer

Implementation lives under `src/`; build/run via
`scripts/run-ml.sh <file.ml>` which delegates to
`sw-cor24-ocaml` and strips the Pascal runtime's source-echo
prefix so downstream output is the program's output only.

## Smoke baseline

`tuplet_build_skeleton` -- runs `src/lex_main.ml` and verifies
the cleaned output is `tuplet-lexer skeleton\n`.

```
REG_RS_DATA_DIR=work/reg-rs reg-rs run -p tuplet_build_skeleton
```

## Source-of-truth references

- `docs/grammar.md` -- what the lexer must produce (token shape,
  Unicode aliases).
- `docs/kernel.md` -- the dynamic-literal-registry contract:
  `Lexer.add_literal` (or equivalent) lets the parser register
  new literal tokens at runtime as `syntax` declarations are
  processed.

## Lexing model: byte stream

The lexer is a byte-stream consumer. Input arrives via `getc`
(one byte at a time, 0..255); output token payload bytes are
emitted via `putc` (one byte at a time). This matches the
host's natural API and Forth's `KEY` / `EMIT` convention -- the
runtime is a byte-oriented system end-to-end.

### EOF sentinel

`getc` blocks at end of input rather than returning a sentinel.
The lexer uses `\x03` (ETX, ASCII 3) as the in-band EOF marker.
Every fixture file ends with one ETX byte; the lexer's main
loop returns when `getc` yields 3.

The choice of ETX is arbitrary but stable: it is not a valid
character anywhere in Tuplet source and survives shell variable
round-tripping (which would strip `\x00`).

### Driver

`scripts/run-lexer-fixture.sh <fixture.input>` feeds a fixture
file's bytes via `OCAML_STDIN`, runs `src/lexer.ml`, strips the
Pascal runtime's source-echo prefixes, and prints just the
emitted tokens. reg-rs baselines drive this script per fixture.

## Token representation

The PoC uses idiomatic OCaml variants -- now supported upstream
since `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#2` was implemented (see commit
`17732be` on `feat/wip`):

```ocaml
type token =
  | TIdent of string
  | TInt of int
  | TPct of int
  | TLArrow
  | TRArrow
  | TLParen
  | TRParen
  | TLBrace
  | TRBrace
  | TComma
  | TUnderscore
  | THash of string
  | TLiteral of string
  | TEOF
  | TMint
```

Constructor payloads carry the relevant data; nullary
constructors have none. Dispatch via `match`.

### Dump format

`src/lexer.ml`'s `dump_tok` writes each token on its own line
via `putc` byte-by-byte: a fixed-width prefix for the kind
(stored as an `int list` of byte values, e.g. `[73; 78; 84;
32; 32; 32; 32]` is `"INT    "`), the payload bytes (for
`THash`) or the integer rendered as decimal digits (for
`TInt`/`TPct`), and a final `putc 10` for LF. Examples:

```
HASH    hello world
INT    42
PCT    50
MINUS
UNK     <byte-int>
EOF
```

### Numeric lexing

Digits accumulate into the integer directly via `n * 10 + (b -
48)` while `is_digit b` holds. The terminating non-digit byte
is returned alongside the number; the main loop threads it
back as the next token's leading byte (one-byte lookahead via
a `pre` parameter, with `0` as the "no pending byte" sentinel
since `0x00` is not a valid Tuplet source byte). If the
post-digit byte is `%` (37) the token is `TPct`, otherwise
`TInt` and the lookahead byte starts the next token.

## Multi-byte tokens (`<-`, `->`)

Lookahead-driven. After reading `<` (60), peek the next byte;
if it's `-` (45) emit `TLArrow`. Otherwise `<` is unknown and
the lookahead byte is threaded back as the next token's
leading byte.

`-` is now lookahead-driven too: read the next byte; if it's
`>` (62) emit `TRArrow`. Otherwise the token is `TMinus` and
the read byte is the next leading byte. The parser later
folds `TMinus` followed by `TInt` into a negative literal.

## Single-byte punctuation

| Byte | Char | Token        |
|------|------|--------------|
| 40   | `(`  | `TLParen`    |
| 41   | `)`  | `TRParen`    |
| 123  | `{`  | `TLBrace`    |
| 125  | `}`  | `TRBrace`    |
| 44   | `,`  | `TComma`     |
| 95   | `_`  | `TUnderscore`|
| 42   | `*`  | `TMint`      |

## Source-ingestion vs runtime-input gotcha

`getc` reads from the SAME UART stream that feeds source code
to the runtime. After source ingestion completes (EOT, 0x04),
runtime input begins. **The lexer driver `let _ = ... lex_loop
...` MUST be the last top-level statement** in the file: any
top-level statements after it would still be in the source
stream, and `getc` would consume their bytes instead of
runtime input. Discovered the hard way in step 005.

## OCaml-subset notes (current)

The `sw-cor24-ocaml` interpreter, since the recent fixes,
supports enough of OCaml to host nontrivial parser code
naturally. Confirmed:

- **Top-level `let X = E` declarations** persist across lines
  (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#3`).
- **User-defined variant types** with payload constructors
  (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#2`).
- **Multi-file modules**: filename `math.ml` becomes module
  `Math`, accessed cross-file as `Math.add`. Multiple files
  passed as args: `run-ocaml.sh math.ml main.ml`. Top-level
  `let` in each file becomes a member of that module.
- **String escapes (`\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`)** and **3+ element
  tuples** (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#4`).
- Function-shorthand `let f x = ...` works.
- `let rec`, lists, pairs, options, pattern matching, qualified
  names (`List.length`, `List.rev`).
- `print_endline`, `print_int`, `string_of_int`, string concat
  via `^`, `int_of_string`.
- Byte I/O: `getc : unit -> int`, `putc : int -> unit`,
  `read_line : unit -> string`.

The Tuplet lexer chooses the byte-stream `getc`/`putc` path
because it matches the host's natural API and the Forth runtime
target -- not because of a missing string-stdlib feature.

## Open upstream limitations

These are absent; the lexer's byte-stream design avoids needing
them, but they would simplify future work:

- **`String.get` / `s.[i]` / `String.sub` / `Char.chr` /
  `Char.code` / `print_string`** -- per-character string access
  and char/int interop. The lexer side-steps the gap by reading
  bytes via `getc` and emitting via `putc`, never assembling
  strings from bytes nor pulling characters out of strings.
- **No string literals in match patterns.** `match x with "foo"
  -> ...` does not parse. With variants available, the lexer
  uses variant constructors for tag dispatch instead.
- **No `(* ... *)` block comments.** OCaml host code uses no
  comments for now.

`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml#4` (string `\n` escapes; 3+ element
tuples) was actually resolved by upstream fixes verified via
re-probing today; the issue may not yet be marked closed but
the runtime accepts both.

## Implementation pattern (current)

Each source file under `src/` is a self-contained program in
the natural OCaml style: top-level `type ...` declaration(s),
top-level `let ...` bindings, then `let _ = ...` to drive the
side-effects of the main flow. Multi-line, with each top-level
declaration on its own line. See `src/token_test.ml` for a
reference shape.

Match arms must currently fit on one line (no multi-line
`match | ... | ...`); single-line dispatch via `match x with
PAT1 -> ... | PAT2 -> ...` is the idiom even when the arm
list is long.

There is no module system / cross-file import. Each `.ml` file
is its own program. Code that several test files would share
is duplicated for now.

content.content:
1	# Tuplet -- Product Requirements Document
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tuplet is an experimental infix programming language in which
6	expressions can emit multiple named values that flow directly into
7	other expressions. It combines infix readability with stack-based
8	execution semantics: tuple variables are first-class producers of
9	fixed-arity value streams, verbs are multi-output, and call-site
10	argument lists concatenate those streams without requiring the caller
11	to overload or repackage. The PoC compiles to Forth and runs on the
12	COR24 Forth runtime.
13	
14	## Goals
15	
16	- First-class named tuple bundles with a declared arity
17	  (e.g. `coord2` produces 2 values, `point4` produces 4).
18	- Multi-output verbs (e.g. `max2`, `min2`, `div2`) with explicit
19	  arity in their name.
20	- Multi-value destructuring assignment: `a, b <- coord2`.
21	- Call-site splicing: `plot(coord2 Red 50%)` expands to four
22	  arguments without plot being overloaded.
23	- Static arity checking at compile time -- every expression has a
24	  known output arity, every call has a known input arity.
25	- Lowering to Forth, exploiting its native multi-output stack words.
26	- ASCII surface syntax as the primary form, with Unicode aliases
27	  accepted for authoring comfort.
28	
29	## Non-goals (initial PoC)
30	
31	- Hindley-Milner type inference. Types are nominal/positional only.
32	- Modules, functors, namespaces.
33	- Exceptions or effect tracking.
34	- Garbage-collected heap. Values are stack cells.
35	- Floating point beyond percent literals (`50%` = 0.5 internally,
36	  represented as the runtime sees fit).
37	- Polymorphism, typeclasses, implicits.
38	- Separate compilation; the PoC is whole-program.
39	
40	## Core concepts
41	
42	**Tuple variable.** A name suffixed with an integer arity. Using the
43	name in expression position produces that many values on the stack.
44	
45	```
46	coord2 <- 3, 9
47	```
48	
49	**Verb signature.** Written with the mapping operator (Unicode
50	`---*` / ASCII `->`). Input and output value lists are parenthesized.
51	
52	```
53	plot (x y color transparency) -> (success)
54	coord2 () -> (x y)
55	```
56	
57	**Multi-value assignment.** LHS may be a comma-separated list of
58	names matching the RHS arity.
59	
60	```
61	a, b <- coord2
62	q, r <- a max2 b
63	integer, fractional <- a div2 b
64	```
65	
66	**Call-site splicing.** In a call, each argument contributes its
67	own arity to the callee's input list. Splicing is the default; a
68	tuple variable is spread, not boxed.
69	
70	```
71	success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
72	```
73	
74	Here `coord2` spreads to two values; `Red` and `50%` are one each;
75	`plot` consumes four inputs total and produces one.
76	
77	## Surface syntax
78	
79	Tuplet has two syntactic surfaces:
80	
81	- **ASCII (primary).** Easy to type, parse, and diff. File
82	  extension `.tup`.
83	- **Unicode (aliases).** Matches the notation in
84	  `docs/research.txt` -- `<-` is `<-` (Unicode `left-arrow`), `->`
85	  is the heavy mapping arrow, `(` `)` are shell-bracket glyphs,
86	  `max2` has a subscript-2 glyph form, etc.
87	
88	The full grammar lives in `docs/grammar.md` (next step).
89	
90	## Example programs
91	
92	```
93	# Tuple init and destructuring
94	coord2 <- 3, 9
95	a, b <- coord2
96	
97	# Multi-output verb returning max then min
98	q, r <- 3 max2 5
99	
100	# Integer and fractional parts of division
101	integer, fractional <- 7 div2 3
102	
103	# Call-site splice with a 2-arity bundle
104	success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
105	
106	# Signature-first style: declare then use
107	plot (x y color transparency) -> (success)
108	coord2 <- 3, 9
109	success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
110	```
111	
112	## Implementation targets
113	
114	- **Parser host:** the integer-subset OCaml interpreter at
115	  `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml`. The Tuplet parser is written in the
116	  OCaml subset that interpreter supports (ints, bools, strings,
117	  lists, pairs, options, pattern matching, `let rec`, `read_line`,
118	  `print_endline`).
119	- **Runtime target:** the DTC Forth at `sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth`,
120	  running under the COR24 emulator (`cor24-run`).
121	- **Test harness:** `reg-rs` golden-output regression tests, covering
122	  both parser behaviour (AST dumps, arity errors) and end-to-end
123	  Forth execution of generated code.
124	- **Pipeline:** source `.tup` -> tokens -> AST -> arity-checked AST
125	  -> stack IR -> Forth source -> `cor24-run` UART output.
126	
127	## Success criteria
128	
129	The PoC is successful when:
130	
131	1. The canonical sample programs parse without error.
132	2. Arity mismatches (wrong LHS count, wrong call arity) are
133	   detected and reported with a location.
134	3. A stack IR is emitted from valid ASTs.
135	4. A small interpreter evaluates the IR and produces expected output
136	   for arithmetic, destructuring, and multi-output verbs.
137	5. Generated Forth runs under `cor24-run` and produces the same
138	   result as the interpreter.
139	6. `reg-rs` captures golden baselines for 6 or more example
140	   programs, all passing.
141	
142	## Risks
143	
144	- **Unicode parsing.** The integer-subset OCaml host has limited
145	  string/char facilities; mixing UTF-8 glyphs with ASCII requires
146	  careful lexer design. Mitigation: author tests and core examples
147	  in ASCII; treat Unicode as an optional alias pass.
148	- **Splicing ambiguity.** Default splicing means a tuple variable
149	  always spreads. Passing a tuple as a single value (e.g. a future
150	  higher-order form) has no syntax yet; this is a deferred design
151	  question, flagged in the grammar doc.
152	- **Parser-host limits.** `sw-cor24-ocaml` is an integer-subset
153	  interpreter. Missing features (e.g., efficient dictionaries,
154	  large-string handling) will be filed as upstream issues against
155	  `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml`, not worked around here.
156	- **Runtime-host limits.** COR24 ISA and Forth word set constraints
157	  may force IR changes. Missing words or bugs will be filed as
158	  upstream issues against `sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth`.
159	- **Scope creep.** The PoC deliberately omits typing, modules, and
160	  exceptions. Pressure to add any of these should be deflected to a
161	  future saga, not mixed into the Phase 0-8 arc.

file.content:
# Tuplet -- Product Requirements Document

## Overview

Tuplet is an experimental infix programming language in which
expressions can emit multiple named values that flow directly into
other expressions. It combines infix readability with stack-based
execution semantics: tuple variables are first-class producers of
fixed-arity value streams, verbs are multi-output, and call-site
argument lists concatenate those streams without requiring the caller
to overload or repackage. The PoC compiles to Forth and runs on the
COR24 Forth runtime.

## Goals

- First-class named tuple bundles with a declared arity
  (e.g. `coord2` produces 2 values, `point4` produces 4).
- Multi-output verbs (e.g. `max2`, `min2`, `div2`) with explicit
  arity in their name.
- Multi-value destructuring assignment: `a, b <- coord2`.
- Call-site splicing: `plot(coord2 Red 50%)` expands to four
  arguments without plot being overloaded.
- Static arity checking at compile time -- every expression has a
  known output arity, every call has a known input arity.
- Lowering to Forth, exploiting its native multi-output stack words.
- ASCII surface syntax as the primary form, with Unicode aliases
  accepted for authoring comfort.

## Non-goals (initial PoC)

- Hindley-Milner type inference. Types are nominal/positional only.
- Modules, functors, namespaces.
- Exceptions or effect tracking.
- Garbage-collected heap. Values are stack cells.
- Floating point beyond percent literals (`50%` = 0.5 internally,
  represented as the runtime sees fit).
- Polymorphism, typeclasses, implicits.
- Separate compilation; the PoC is whole-program.

## Core concepts

**Tuple variable.** A name suffixed with an integer arity. Using the
name in expression position produces that many values on the stack.

```
coord2 <- 3, 9
```

**Verb signature.** Written with the mapping operator (Unicode
`---*` / ASCII `->`). Input and output value lists are parenthesized.

```
plot (x y color transparency) -> (success)
coord2 () -> (x y)
```

**Multi-value assignment.** LHS may be a comma-separated list of
names matching the RHS arity.

```
a, b <- coord2
q, r <- a max2 b
integer, fractional <- a div2 b
```

**Call-site splicing.** In a call, each argument contributes its
own arity to the callee's input list. Splicing is the default; a
tuple variable is spread, not boxed.

```
success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
```

Here `coord2` spreads to two values; `Red` and `50%` are one each;
`plot` consumes four inputs total and produces one.

## Surface syntax

Tuplet has two syntactic surfaces:

- **ASCII (primary).** Easy to type, parse, and diff. File
  extension `.tup`.
- **Unicode (aliases).** Matches the notation in
  `docs/research.txt` -- `<-` is `<-` (Unicode `left-arrow`), `->`
  is the heavy mapping arrow, `(` `)` are shell-bracket glyphs,
  `max2` has a subscript-2 glyph form, etc.

The full grammar lives in `docs/grammar.md` (next step).

## Example programs

```
# Tuple init and destructuring
coord2 <- 3, 9
a, b <- coord2

# Multi-output verb returning max then min
q, r <- 3 max2 5

# Integer and fractional parts of division
integer, fractional <- 7 div2 3

# Call-site splice with a 2-arity bundle
success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)

# Signature-first style: declare then use
plot (x y color transparency) -> (success)
coord2 <- 3, 9
success <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
```

## Implementation targets

- **Parser host:** the integer-subset OCaml interpreter at
  `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml`. The Tuplet parser is written in the
  OCaml subset that interpreter supports (ints, bools, strings,
  lists, pairs, options, pattern matching, `let rec`, `read_line`,
  `print_endline`).
- **Runtime target:** the DTC Forth at `sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth`,
  running under the COR24 emulator (`cor24-run`).
- **Test harness:** `reg-rs` golden-output regression tests, covering
  both parser behaviour (AST dumps, arity errors) and end-to-end
  Forth execution of generated code.
- **Pipeline:** source `.tup` -> tokens -> AST -> arity-checked AST
  -> stack IR -> Forth source -> `cor24-run` UART output.

## Success criteria

The PoC is successful when:

1. The canonical sample programs parse without error.
2. Arity mismatches (wrong LHS count, wrong call arity) are
   detected and reported with a location.
3. A stack IR is emitted from valid ASTs.
4. A small interpreter evaluates the IR and produces expected output
   for arithmetic, destructuring, and multi-output verbs.
5. Generated Forth runs under `cor24-run` and produces the same
   result as the interpreter.
6. `reg-rs` captures golden baselines for 6 or more example
   programs, all passing.

## Risks

- **Unicode parsing.** The integer-subset OCaml host has limited
  string/char facilities; mixing UTF-8 glyphs with ASCII requires
  careful lexer design. Mitigation: author tests and core examples
  in ASCII; treat Unicode as an optional alias pass.
- **Splicing ambiguity.** Default splicing means a tuple variable
  always spreads. Passing a tuple as a single value (e.g. a future
  higher-order form) has no syntax yet; this is a deferred design
  question, flagged in the grammar doc.
- **Parser-host limits.** `sw-cor24-ocaml` is an integer-subset
  interpreter. Missing features (e.g., efficient dictionaries,
  large-string handling) will be filed as upstream issues against
  `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml`, not worked around here.
- **Runtime-host limits.** COR24 ISA and Forth word set constraints
  may force IR changes. Missing words or bugs will be filed as
  upstream issues against `sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth`.
- **Scope creep.** The PoC deliberately omits typing, modules, and
  exceptions. Pressure to add any of these should be deflected to a
  future saga, not mixed into the Phase 0-8 arc.
