1	# Tuplet -- Design
2	
3	Low-level design for the Tuplet PoC: compiler pipeline, AST, stack
4	IR, and the builtin verb registry. Complements `docs/prd.md` (what
5	Tuplet is) and `docs/grammar.md` (the surface syntax).
6	
7	## Pipeline
8	
9	```
10	source.tup
11	   |
12	   v
13	+------+     +--------+     +-----+     +-----------------+
14	| lex  | --> | parse  | --> | AST | --> | name resolution |
15	+------+     +--------+     +-----+     +-----------------+
16	                                              |
17	                                              v
18	                                        +---------------+
19	                                        | arity checker |
20	                                        +---------------+
21	                                              |
22	                                              v
23	                                          +--------+
24	                                          | IR gen |
25	                                          +--------+
26	                                              |
27	                                              v
28	                            +-----+-----+-----+-----+
29	                            |                       |
30	                            v                       v
31	                    +---------------+       +----------------+
32	                    | interpreter   |       | Forth emitter  |
33	                    | (cross check) |       | (runtime path) |
34	                    +---------------+       +----------------+
35	                                                     |
36	                                                     v
37	                                             forth generated code
38	                                                     |
39	                                                     v
40	                                              cor24-run (emulator)
41	```
42	
43	The interpreter backend exists mainly as a reference oracle for
44	tests: every program that compiles to Forth also runs in the
45	in-process interpreter, and reg-rs compares the two outputs to
46	catch emitter regressions.
47	
48	## AST types
49	
50	Primary form (idiomatic OCaml):
51	
52	```ocaml
53	type name = string
54	
55	type op =
56	  | OpAdd | OpSub | OpMul
57	  | OpMax  | OpMin  | OpDiv
58	  | OpMax2 | OpMin2 | OpDiv2
59	
60	type expr =
61	  | EInt    of int
62	  | EBool   of bool
63	  | EPct    of int            (* 0..100 *)
64	  | ESymbol of string         (* Red, Green, Blue, ... *)
65	  | EName   of name
66	  | EBinary of expr * op * expr
67	  | ECall   of name * expr list
68	  | ETuple  of expr list      (* comma-separated RHS, e.g., 3, 9 *)
69	
70	type pattern =
71	  | PName  of name            (* single lvalue: scalar or tuple-var *)
72	  | PTuple of name list       (* destructuring: a, b, c *)
73	
74	type field = { f_name : name }
75	
76	type sigdecl = {
77	  s_name    : name;
78	  s_inputs  : field list;
79	  s_outputs : field list;
80	}
81	
82	type tupdecl = {
83	  t_name   : name;            (* e.g. "coord2" *)
84	  t_fields : field list;      (* length must match name's arity *)
85	}
86	
87	type stmt =
88	  | SDecl      of tupdecl     (* coord2 -> (x y) *)
89	  | SSignature of sigdecl     (* plot(x y color c?) -> (success?) *)
90	  | SAssign    of pattern * expr
91	  | SExpr      of expr
92	  | SComment   of string
93	
94	type program = stmt list
95	```
96	
97	Name resolution produces a symbol table mapping each name to one of:
98	`SymScalar`, `SymTuple of arity`, `SymVerb of in_arity * out_arity`.
99	
100	### OCaml-subset compatibility (sw-cor24-ocaml)
101	
102	`sw-cor24-ocaml` today supports: ints, bools, strings, lists, pairs,
103	options, pattern matching, `let rec`, qualified names. It does **not
104	document** user-defined algebraic data types or records.
105	
106	If user-defined variants turn out to be unavailable, fall back to a
107	tagged-tuple encoding:
108	
109	```ocaml
110	(* expr as ("tag", payload) pairs; payload shape per tag. *)
111	let e_int n          = ("EInt",    (n, 0, ""))
112	let e_bool b         = ("EBool",   (0, (if b then 1 else 0), ""))
113	let e_pct p          = ("EPct",    (p, 0, ""))
114	let e_symbol s       = ("ESymbol", (0, 0, s))
115	let e_name s         = ("EName",   (0, 0, s))
116	(* EBinary / ECall / ETuple encoded as nested tagged tuples. *)
117	```
118	
119	Pattern-match by the tag string in the first slot. This is verbose
120	but fits the subset we have.
121	
122	**Upstream dependency.** If custom variants are needed for
123	readability at scale, file an issue at `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml`
124	asking for user-defined sum types; do not switch away from OCaml
125	to avoid the limitation. Track under `docs/tooling-smoke.md` -> 
126	Blocked once filed.
127	
128	## Stack IR
129	
130	A linear list of instructions executed on an operand stack of value
131	cells (int-tagged for the PoC).
132	
133	```ocaml
134	type instr =
135	  | IPushInt    of int
136	  | IPushBool   of bool
137	  | IPushPct    of int             (* 0..100 *)
138	  | IPushSymbol of string          (* e.g. "Red" *)
139	  | ILoad       of name            (* scalar load: ( -- x ) *)
140	  | IStore      of name            (* scalar store: ( x -- ) *)
141	  | ILoadTuple  of name * int      (* ( -- v1 v2 ... vN ) *)
142	  | IStoreTuple of name * int      (* ( v1 v2 ... vN -- ) *)
143	  | ICall       of name * int      (* N inputs consumed *)
144	  | IBinOp      of op
145	```
146	
147	### Stack effects
148	
149	| Instruction              | Effect                                 |
150	|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
151	| `IPushInt n`             | `( -- n )`                             |
152	| `IPushBool b`            | `( -- b )`                             |
153	| `IPushPct p`             | `( -- p )`  -- percent as int 0..100   |
154	| `IPushSymbol s`          | `( -- sym )` -- resolved at emit time  |
155	| `ILoad x`                | `( -- v )`                             |
156	| `IStore x`               | `( v -- )`                             |
157	| `ILoadTuple (t, n)`      | `( -- v1 v2 ... vn )` -- topmost last  |
158	| `IStoreTuple (t, n)`     | `( v1 v2 ... vn -- )` -- topmost last  |
159	| `ICall (v, n)`           | `( a1 ... an -- r1 ... rk )`, k from   |
160	|                          | verb's declared output arity            |
161	| `IBinOp op`              | `( a b -- r1 ... rk )`, k from op arity |
162	
163	**Tuple order convention.** Fields are pushed in declaration order:
164	for `coord2 -> (x y)`, after `ILoadTuple ("coord2", 2)` the stack
165	top is `y` and `x` is directly below. `IStoreTuple` consumes them in
166	the same reading order (so the source `coord2 <- 3, 9` pushes 3
167	then 9, then stores).
168	
169	## Builtin verb registry
170	
171	Shipped with the PoC. All arities are static.
172	
173	| Name    | In | Out | Notes                                    |
174	|---------|----|-----|------------------------------------------|
175	| `+`     | 2  | 1   | Integer add.                             |
176	| `-`     | 2  | 1   | Integer sub.                             |
177	| `*`     | 2  | 1   | Integer mul.                             |
178	| `max`   | 2  | 1   | `max(a, b)`                              |
179	| `min`   | 2  | 1   | `min(a, b)`                              |
180	| `div`   | 2  | 1   | Integer division, truncates.             |
181	| `max2`  | 2  | 2   | Returns `(hi, lo)` -- first is >=.       |
182	| `min2`  | 2  | 2   | Returns `(lo, hi)` -- first is <=.       |
183	| `div2`  | 2  | 2   | Returns `(quotient, remainder)`.         |
184	| `plot`  | 4  | 1   | `(x y color c?) -> (success?)`; the PoC  |
185	|         |    |     | backend prints the args and returns 1.   |
186	
187	Colors (`Red`, `Green`, `Blue`) are ESymbol literals resolved to
188	small integers at emit time (e.g. `Red = 82` -- ASCII 'R').
189	A stable symbol->int table lives alongside the Forth emitter.
190	
191	## Worked example
192	
193	Source:
194	
195	```
196	coord2 -> (x y)
197	coord2 <- 3, 9
198	success? <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
199	```
200	
201	### AST (pseudo-OCaml)
202	
203	```ocaml
204	[
205	  SDecl { t_name = "coord2"; t_fields = [{f_name="x"}; {f_name="y"}] };
206	  SAssign (PName "coord2",
207	           ETuple [EInt 3; EInt 9]);
208	  SAssign (PName "success?",
209	           ECall ("plot",
210	                  [EName "coord2";
211	                   ESymbol "Red";
212	                   EPct 50]));
213	]
214	```
215	
216	### Stack IR
217	
218	```
219	; SDecl coord2 -> (x y)            -- no IR emitted; just updates
220	;                                     the symbol table (SymTuple 2).
221	
222	; coord2 <- 3, 9
223	IPushInt 3
224	IPushInt 9
225	IStoreTuple ("coord2", 2)
226	
227	; success? <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
228	ILoadTuple  ("coord2", 2)          ; pushes 3 then 9
229	IPushSymbol "Red"                  ; resolves to int at emit
230	IPushPct    50
231	ICall       ("plot", 4)            ; consumes 4, produces 1
232	IStore      "success?"
233	```
234	
235	Arity check for the call: `coord2` (2) + `Red` (1) + `50%` (1) = 4,
236	which matches `plot`'s declared in-arity. OK.
237	
238	### Expected Forth (detailed rules in `docs/lowering.md`)
239	
240	```
241	\ Per-tuple: backing variables and ! / @ words.
242	VARIABLE coord2-x
243	VARIABLE coord2-y
244	: coord2!  ( x y -- )    coord2-y ! coord2-x ! ;
245	: coord2@  ( -- x y )    coord2-x @ coord2-y @ ;
246	
247	VARIABLE success?q      \ scalar backing
248	: success?!  ( v -- )   success?q ! ;
249	: success?@  ( -- v )   success?q @ ;
250	
251	\ Program body.
252	3 9 coord2!
253	coord2@ 82 50 plot success?!
254	```
255	
256	(`82` is the symbol table entry for `Red`; `50` is the percent
257	literal as int; `plot` is a builtin word provided by the Tuplet
258	runtime layer that sits on top of `sw-cor24-forth`.)
259	
260	## Open questions
261	
262	- **Passing a tuple as one value.** No syntax yet; see `docs/grammar.md`
263	  splicing TODO. Design deferred.
264	- **Negative percent / percent arithmetic.** Current PoC treats `%`
265	  only as a literal constructor; there is no `(50% + 25%)` form.
266	- **String / text output.** `plot`'s mock output assumes the runtime
267	  has a way to print, which for the PoC is `.` or `EMIT` in Forth.
268	  `sw-cor24-forth` has both. Nothing upstream needed.
269	- **Boolean return of `plot`.** Forth has no native bool type;
270	  represent as `0 = false`, `-1 = true`, matching Forth convention.
271	
272	## Upstream dependencies
273	
274	None filed yet. If user-defined sum types or records turn out to be
275	required by the parser implementation, file at
276	`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml` and record here.
277

# Tuplet -- Design

Low-level design for the Tuplet PoC: compiler pipeline, AST, stack
IR, and the builtin verb registry. Complements `docs/prd.md` (what
Tuplet is) and `docs/grammar.md` (the surface syntax).

## Pipeline

```
source.tup
   |
   v
+------+     +--------+     +-----+     +-----------------+
| lex  | --> | parse  | --> | AST | --> | name resolution |
+------+     +--------+     +-----+     +-----------------+
                                              |
                                              v
                                        +---------------+
                                        | arity checker |
                                        +---------------+
                                              |
                                              v
                                          +--------+
                                          | IR gen |
                                          +--------+
                                              |
                                              v
                            +-----+-----+-----+-----+
                            |                       |
                            v                       v
                    +---------------+       +----------------+
                    | interpreter   |       | Forth emitter  |
                    | (cross check) |       | (runtime path) |
                    +---------------+       +----------------+
                                                     |
                                                     v
                                             forth generated code
                                                     |
                                                     v
                                              cor24-run (emulator)
```

The interpreter backend exists mainly as a reference oracle for
tests: every program that compiles to Forth also runs in the
in-process interpreter, and reg-rs compares the two outputs to
catch emitter regressions.

## AST types

Primary form (idiomatic OCaml):

```ocaml
type name = string

type op =
  | OpAdd | OpSub | OpMul
  | OpMax  | OpMin  | OpDiv
  | OpMax2 | OpMin2 | OpDiv2

type expr =
  | EInt    of int
  | EBool   of bool
  | EPct    of int            (* 0..100 *)
  | ESymbol of string         (* Red, Green, Blue, ... *)
  | EName   of name
  | EBinary of expr * op * expr
  | ECall   of name * expr list
  | ETuple  of expr list      (* comma-separated RHS, e.g., 3, 9 *)

type pattern =
  | PName  of name            (* single lvalue: scalar or tuple-var *)
  | PTuple of name list       (* destructuring: a, b, c *)

type field = { f_name : name }

type sigdecl = {
  s_name    : name;
  s_inputs  : field list;
  s_outputs : field list;
}

type tupdecl = {
  t_name   : name;            (* e.g. "coord2" *)
  t_fields : field list;      (* length must match name's arity *)
}

type stmt =
  | SDecl      of tupdecl     (* coord2 -> (x y) *)
  | SSignature of sigdecl     (* plot(x y color c?) -> (success?) *)
  | SAssign    of pattern * expr
  | SExpr      of expr
  | SComment   of string

type program = stmt list
```

Name resolution produces a symbol table mapping each name to one of:
`SymScalar`, `SymTuple of arity`, `SymVerb of in_arity * out_arity`.

### OCaml-subset compatibility (sw-cor24-ocaml)

`sw-cor24-ocaml` today supports: ints, bools, strings, lists, pairs,
options, pattern matching, `let rec`, qualified names. It does **not
document** user-defined algebraic data types or records.

If user-defined variants turn out to be unavailable, fall back to a
tagged-tuple encoding:

```ocaml
(* expr as ("tag", payload) pairs; payload shape per tag. *)
let e_int n          = ("EInt",    (n, 0, ""))
let e_bool b         = ("EBool",   (0, (if b then 1 else 0), ""))
let e_pct p          = ("EPct",    (p, 0, ""))
let e_symbol s       = ("ESymbol", (0, 0, s))
let e_name s         = ("EName",   (0, 0, s))
(* EBinary / ECall / ETuple encoded as nested tagged tuples. *)
```

Pattern-match by the tag string in the first slot. This is verbose
but fits the subset we have.

**Upstream dependency.** If custom variants are needed for
readability at scale, file an issue at `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml`
asking for user-defined sum types; do not switch away from OCaml
to avoid the limitation. Track under `docs/tooling-smoke.md` -> 
Blocked once filed.

## Stack IR

A linear list of instructions executed on an operand stack of value
cells (int-tagged for the PoC).

```ocaml
type instr =
  | IPushInt    of int
  | IPushBool   of bool
  | IPushPct    of int             (* 0..100 *)
  | IPushSymbol of string          (* e.g. "Red" *)
  | ILoad       of name            (* scalar load: ( -- x ) *)
  | IStore      of name            (* scalar store: ( x -- ) *)
  | ILoadTuple  of name * int      (* ( -- v1 v2 ... vN ) *)
  | IStoreTuple of name * int      (* ( v1 v2 ... vN -- ) *)
  | ICall       of name * int      (* N inputs consumed *)
  | IBinOp      of op
```

### Stack effects

| Instruction              | Effect                                 |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| `IPushInt n`             | `( -- n )`                             |
| `IPushBool b`            | `( -- b )`                             |
| `IPushPct p`             | `( -- p )`  -- percent as int 0..100   |
| `IPushSymbol s`          | `( -- sym )` -- resolved at emit time  |
| `ILoad x`                | `( -- v )`                             |
| `IStore x`               | `( v -- )`                             |
| `ILoadTuple (t, n)`      | `( -- v1 v2 ... vn )` -- topmost last  |
| `IStoreTuple (t, n)`     | `( v1 v2 ... vn -- )` -- topmost last  |
| `ICall (v, n)`           | `( a1 ... an -- r1 ... rk )`, k from   |
|                          | verb's declared output arity            |
| `IBinOp op`              | `( a b -- r1 ... rk )`, k from op arity |

**Tuple order convention.** Fields are pushed in declaration order:
for `coord2 -> (x y)`, after `ILoadTuple ("coord2", 2)` the stack
top is `y` and `x` is directly below. `IStoreTuple` consumes them in
the same reading order (so the source `coord2 <- 3, 9` pushes 3
then 9, then stores).

## Builtin verb registry

Shipped with the PoC. All arities are static.

| Name    | In | Out | Notes                                    |
|---------|----|-----|------------------------------------------|
| `+`     | 2  | 1   | Integer add.                             |
| `-`     | 2  | 1   | Integer sub.                             |
| `*`     | 2  | 1   | Integer mul.                             |
| `max`   | 2  | 1   | `max(a, b)`                              |
| `min`   | 2  | 1   | `min(a, b)`                              |
| `div`   | 2  | 1   | Integer division, truncates.             |
| `max2`  | 2  | 2   | Returns `(hi, lo)` -- first is >=.       |
| `min2`  | 2  | 2   | Returns `(lo, hi)` -- first is <=.       |
| `div2`  | 2  | 2   | Returns `(quotient, remainder)`.         |
| `plot`  | 4  | 1   | `(x y color c?) -> (success?)`; the PoC  |
|         |    |     | backend prints the args and returns 1.   |

Colors (`Red`, `Green`, `Blue`) are ESymbol literals resolved to
small integers at emit time (e.g. `Red = 82` -- ASCII 'R').
A stable symbol->int table lives alongside the Forth emitter.

## Worked example

Source:

```
coord2 -> (x y)
coord2 <- 3, 9
success? <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
```

### AST (pseudo-OCaml)

```ocaml
[
  SDecl { t_name = "coord2"; t_fields = [{f_name="x"}; {f_name="y"}] };
  SAssign (PName "coord2",
           ETuple [EInt 3; EInt 9]);
  SAssign (PName "success?",
           ECall ("plot",
                  [EName "coord2";
                   ESymbol "Red";
                   EPct 50]));
]
```

### Stack IR

```
; SDecl coord2 -> (x y)            -- no IR emitted; just updates
;                                     the symbol table (SymTuple 2).

; coord2 <- 3, 9
IPushInt 3
IPushInt 9
IStoreTuple ("coord2", 2)

; success? <- plot(coord2 Red 50%)
ILoadTuple  ("coord2", 2)          ; pushes 3 then 9
IPushSymbol "Red"                  ; resolves to int at emit
IPushPct    50
ICall       ("plot", 4)            ; consumes 4, produces 1
IStore      "success?"
```

Arity check for the call: `coord2` (2) + `Red` (1) + `50%` (1) = 4,
which matches `plot`'s declared in-arity. OK.

### Expected Forth (detailed rules in `docs/lowering.md`)

```
\ Per-tuple: backing variables and ! / @ words.
VARIABLE coord2-x
VARIABLE coord2-y
: coord2!  ( x y -- )    coord2-y ! coord2-x ! ;
: coord2@  ( -- x y )    coord2-x @ coord2-y @ ;

VARIABLE success?q      \ scalar backing
: success?!  ( v -- )   success?q ! ;
: success?@  ( -- v )   success?q @ ;

\ Program body.
3 9 coord2!
coord2@ 82 50 plot success?!
```

(`82` is the symbol table entry for `Red`; `50` is the percent
literal as int; `plot` is a builtin word provided by the Tuplet
runtime layer that sits on top of `sw-cor24-forth`.)

## Open questions

- **Passing a tuple as one value.** No syntax yet; see `docs/grammar.md`
  splicing TODO. Design deferred.
- **Negative percent / percent arithmetic.** Current PoC treats `%`
  only as a literal constructor; there is no `(50% + 25%)` form.
- **String / text output.** `plot`'s mock output assumes the runtime
  has a way to print, which for the PoC is `.` or `EMIT` in Forth.
  `sw-cor24-forth` has both. Nothing upstream needed.
- **Boolean return of `plot`.** Forth has no native bool type;
  represent as `0 = false`, `-1 = true`, matching Forth convention.

## Upstream dependencies

None filed yet. If user-defined sum types or records turn out to be
required by the parser implementation, file at
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml` and record here.

content.content:
1	# Tuplet -- Phased Plan
2	
3	> **Architecture source of truth:** `docs/kernel.md`. The
4	> kernel/prelude split decided there shapes every saga below.
5	> If this plan disagrees with `docs/kernel.md`, the kernel doc
6	> wins; raise the disagreement and update this file.
7	>
8	> **PoC milestone:** `docs/poc-goals.md` defines the single
9	> demoable target for this project: a REPL where the user types
10	> a `syntax` declaration that adds `do..while` and uses it. The
11	> milestone lands inside saga 6 (`tuplet-forth-emit`) plus a
12	> small REPL driver -- *not* at the end of saga 8. Sagas 7-8
13	> exist to thicken the language; the PoC proves the mechanism.
14	
15	## Arc summary
16	
17	`tuplet-scaffold` (Phase 0, in-progress) lays down the docs and
18	toolchain wiring. From there, eight follow-up sagas build the
19	PoC end-to-end: lexer -> registry-based parser -> registry-based
20	checker -> stack IR -> reference interpreter -> Forth emitter ->
21	**`lib/std.tup` prelude** -> demos. The seventh saga
22	(`tuplet-prelude`) is what makes Tuplet "mostly written in
23	itself"; the eighth proves it by running demos that depend on
24	the prelude.
25	
26	## Saga index
27	
28	| #  | Saga                          | Phase | One-line goal                                                                | Status        |
29	|----|-------------------------------|-------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
30	| 0  | `tuplet-scaffold`             | 0     | Docs (PRD, grammar, design, lowering, kernel) + toolchain smoke baselines.   | in-progress   |
31	| 1  | `tuplet-lexer`                | 1     | Tokenize `.tup`; surface registry-callback for template literals.            | upcoming      |
32	| 2  | `tuplet-parser`               | 2     | `syntax` registry + longest-match template matcher producing AST.            | upcoming      |
33	| 3  | `tuplet-checker`              | 3     | Name resolution + arity check against the registry; no hardcoded ops.        | upcoming      |
34	| 4  | `tuplet-ir`                   | 4     | AST -> stack IR including `IPrimForth` and anonymous-verb thunks.            | upcoming      |
35	| 5  | `tuplet-interp`               | 5     | Minimal reference interpreter over IR; oracle for emitter cross-checks.      | upcoming      |
36	| 6  | `tuplet-forth-emit`           | 6     | IR -> Forth into `work/generated/*.fs`; cor24-run round-trip via reg-rs.     | upcoming      |
37	| 7  | **`tuplet-prelude`**          | 7     | Write `lib/std.tup`: operators + control flow + tuple helpers in Tuplet.     | upcoming      |
38	| 8  | `tuplet-demos`                | 8     | Example gallery using prelude features only; "remove std.tup -> fail" test.  | upcoming      |
39	
40	## Cross-cutting concerns
41	
42	Every saga must honor these. They are not restated in each saga
43	section.
44	
45	- **Do not edit outside this repo.** Tuplet code, docs, tests,
46	  generated Forth, and reg-rs baselines all live in
47	  `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tuplet`.
48	- **Upstream gaps are GitHub issues, not local workarounds.**
49	  Missing features or bugs in `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml` or
50	  `sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth` are filed via `gh issue create
51	  --repo sw-embed/<repo>`. Record the issue number under the
52	  saga's risks. If it blocks the saga, run `agentrail abort
53	  --reason "blocked on sw-embed/<repo>#<n>"`.
54	- **Push every commit in the same session.** Per CLAUDE.md
55	  section 4. Never defer `git push`.
56	- **Commit the full `.agentrail/` delta with each step.** Stage
57	  everything modified or new under `.agentrail/`, not just the
58	  step you thought you were touching.
59	- **`markdown-checker` clean on every changed `.md`.**
60	- **reg-rs baseline policy.** `*.rgt` (test definition) and
61	  `*.out` (golden output) are tracked; `*.tdb` and `*.lock` are
62	  ignored. New tests live under `work/reg-rs/` via
63	  `REG_RS_DATA_DIR=work/reg-rs reg-rs create ...`.
64	
65	## Per-saga sections
66	
67	### 1. `tuplet-lexer`
68	
69	**Goal.** Tokenize Tuplet source. Hand-written lexer in the
70	OCaml subset hosted on `sw-cor24-ocaml`. Surfaces an interface
71	the parser uses to register new literal tokens during parsing
72	(needed because `syntax` declarations introduce new keywords).
73	
74	**Entrance criteria.**
75	- `tuplet-scaffold` complete on `main`.
76	- `docs/grammar.md`, `docs/kernel.md` present.
77	- `tuplet_ocaml_smoke` reg-rs test green.
78	
79	**Exit criteria.**
80	- `src/lexer.ml` (or equivalent path) tokenizes a `.tup` file
81	  to a list of tokens, dumpable as a deterministic line per
82	  token.
83	- ASCII and Unicode aliases per `docs/grammar.md` table both
84	  produce the same token stream.
85	- Registry-callback hook present: parser can call
86	  `Lexer.add_literal "if"` and a subsequent re-lex of the same
87	  source treats `if` as a literal token.
88	- Reg-rs baselines for: scalar literals, tuple-var names with
89	  arity suffixes, comments, ASCII vs Unicode form, dynamic
90	  registration round-trip.
91	
92	**Key deliverables.**
93	- `src/lexer.ml`, `src/token.ml`.
94	- `tests/lexer/*.tup` + `*.expected.tokens`.
95	- `work/reg-rs/tuplet_lex_*.rgt` + `*.out`.
96	
97	**Primary risks.**
98	- OCaml subset may lack efficient string compare or mutable
99	  hashtables for the literal registry. File upstream issue
100	  against `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml` if so; fall back to assoc
101	  lists in the meantime.
102	- Unicode multi-byte handling. Strategy: keep alias folding
103	  byte-pattern based, narrow set, ASCII-canonical post-fold.
104	
105	### 2. `tuplet-parser`
106	
107	**Goal.** Build AST per `docs/design.md`, with a `syntax`
108	registry and longest-match template matcher. The parser knows
109	no operators or control-flow keywords -- those come from the
110	registry.
111	
112	**Entrance criteria.**
113	- `tuplet-lexer` complete; lexer can register literal tokens.
114	- `docs/kernel.md` `syntax` semantics accepted.
115	
116	**Exit criteria.**
117	- `src/parser.ml` parses kernel forms (`syntax`, `:`, `<-`,
118	  comma, parens, `#`, `_`, `{}`, `prim/forth`) without any
119	  registry entries.
120	- After processing one `syntax` declaration, subsequent code
121	  that matches the template parses to the documented AST.
122	- Longest-match-wins, first-declared-wins-on-ties: tested with
123	  a fixture that registers two overlapping templates.
124	- AST-dump format is deterministic (s-expr or similar).
125	- Reg-rs baselines for: kernel-only programs, template
126	  expansion sanity, ambiguity resolution, error messages on
127	  unknown tokens / unmatched templates.
128	
129	**Key deliverables.**
130	- `src/parser.ml`, `src/ast.ml`, `src/registry.ml`.
131	- `tests/parser/*.tup` + `*.expected.ast`.
132	
133	**Primary risks.**
134	- Template matching in the OCaml subset may need data
135	  structures (priority queue / trie) absent from the host. Plan
136	  for assoc-list fallback; profile only if needed.
137	- Registry mutation during parse complicates error recovery.
138	  Keep parser single-pass; on error, abort and report.
139	
140	### 3. `tuplet-checker`
141	
142	**Goal.** Name resolution and arity checking on the registry-based
143	AST. No hardcoded operators; every check goes through the
144	registry.
145	
146	**Entrance criteria.**
147	- `tuplet-parser` complete; AST + registry stable.
148	
149	**Exit criteria.**
150	- All Arity rules from `docs/grammar.md` enforced for forms
151	  defined in the kernel + any registered `syntax` declarations.
152	- Pass-case fixtures produce a typed-AST dump; fail-case
153	  fixtures produce the documented error message text.
154	- Reg-rs baselines for both pass and fail cases.
155	
156	**Key deliverables.**
157	- `src/checker.ml`.
158	- `tests/checker/pass/*.tup`, `tests/checker/fail/*.tup` +
159	  `*.expected.err`.
160	
161	**Primary risks.**
162	- Slot-arity propagation through nested templates; document
163	  the algorithm in a short note alongside the implementation.
164	
165	### 4. `tuplet-ir`
166	
167	**Goal.** Lower checked AST to the stack IR from
168	`docs/design.md`, extended with `IPrimForth` and anonymous-verb
169	thunks per `docs/kernel.md`.
170	
171	**Entrance criteria.**
172	- `tuplet-checker` complete.
173	
174	**Exit criteria.**
175	- Every IR opcode from the design doc emitted correctly,
176	  including `IPrimForth "WORD"` for raw escape and synthetic
177	  thunks for `{...}`.
178	- Reg-rs baselines for the IR dumps of representative programs
179	  (kernel-only, with prelude templates expanded).
180	
181	**Key deliverables.**
182	- `src/ir.ml`, `src/lower.ml`.
183	- `tests/ir/*.tup` + `*.expected.ir`.
184	
185	**Primary risks.**
186	- IR ergonomics for thunks. `:NONAME` is now available
187	  (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth#5` closed), so thunks lower
188	  directly without the synthetic-name fallback originally
189	  planned.
190	
191	### 5. `tuplet-interp`
192	
193	**Goal.** A minimal reference interpreter over the IR. Used as
194	an oracle for the Forth emitter -- not a target itself.
195	
196	**Entrance criteria.**
197	- `tuplet-ir` complete.
198	
199	**Exit criteria.**
200	- For every program in the test corpus, the interpreter
201	  produces the same output the Forth emitter will be expected
202	  to produce.
203	- Stub for `IPrimForth`: a small allowlist of supported words
204	  (`+`, `*`, `<`, `IF`, etc.) implemented in the interpreter
205	  with mock semantics.
206	
207	**Key deliverables.**
208	- `src/interp.ml`.
209	- `tests/interp/*.tup` + `*.expected.out`.
210	
211	**Primary risks.**
212	- Drift between interpreter and Forth semantics for edge
213	  cases; document divergences explicitly. The Forth output is
214	  authoritative; the interpreter is the cross-check.
215	
216	### 6. `tuplet-forth-emit`
217	
218	**Goal.** Emit Forth from IR per `docs/lowering.md`, into
219	`work/generated/<basename>.fs`. Concatenate kernel `forth.s` +
220	generated file, run under `cor24-run`, capture UART output via
221	reg-rs, compare against the interpreter. **PoC milestone lands
222	here**: the saga's exit also delivers a `tuplet repl` driver
223	sufficient to demonstrate `docs/poc-goals.md`.
224	
225	**Entrance criteria.**
226	- `tuplet-ir` and `tuplet-interp` complete.
227	- `tuplet_forth_smoke` reg-rs test green.
228	
229	**Exit criteria.**
230	- `tuplet compile <file>.tup` writes `work/generated/<file>.fs`.
231	- `tuplet run <file>.tup` runs end-to-end through `cor24-run`
232	  and prints the UART output section.
233	- For every program in the test corpus, interpreter output
234	  matches Forth output (modulo the documented divergence
235	  policy).
236	- Reg-rs baselines compare emitter output against the
237	  interpreter.
238	
239	**Key deliverables.**
240	- `src/emit.ml`.
241	- `bin/tuplet` (the CLI; `tuplet compile`, `tuplet run`,
242	  `tuplet repl`).
243	- `work/generated/` (gitignored output dir).
244	- `work/reg-rs/tuplet_poc_dowhile.{rgt,out}` -- scripted REPL
245	  session per `docs/poc-goals.md` acceptance criteria.
246	- `docs/poc-demo.md` -- the PoC walkthrough.
247	
248	**Primary risks.**
249	- Per-instruction RX/TX trace lines from `cor24-run` shifting
250	  with emulator version; existing grep-preprocess pattern from
251	  `tuplet_forth_smoke` is the template.
252	- Anonymous verbs use kernel `:NONAME` (verified via
253	  `tuplet_forth_noname_smoke`).
254	
255	### 7. `tuplet-prelude` (the bootstrap-proof saga)
256	
257	**Goal.** Write `lib/std.tup`: every operator (`+ - * < = <> <=
258	>= not && ||`), every multi-output verb (`max min div max2 min2
259	div2`), and every control-flow construct (`if/then/else/end`,
260	`while/do/end`, sketch of `match`) defined in Tuplet using only
261	the kernel + earlier prelude entries + `prim/forth` escapes.
262	
263	**Entrance criteria.**
264	- `tuplet-forth-emit` complete; round-trip via reg-rs working.
265	
266	**Exit criteria.**
267	- `lib/std.tup` exists and loads without forward-reference
268	  errors.
269	- A test program using `+`, `if/then/else/end`, `while/do/end`
270	  produces the expected output via the Tuplet emitter, using
271	  *only* prelude features for those constructs.
272	- The prelude file has a header comment listing the load
273	  order; a build-time check fails on forward references.
274	
275	**Key deliverables.**
276	- `lib/std.tup`.
277	- `tests/prelude/*.tup` exercising every prelude form.
278	- A loader / driver that wires `lib/std.tup` into the
279	  compiler's startup sequence.
280	
281	**Primary risks.**
282	- The litmus test in `docs/kernel.md` is wrong somewhere. If a
283	  prelude entry needs a primitive that isn't in `prim/`, that's
284	  evidence the kernel inventory needs a new entry; revisit
285	  `docs/kernel.md` and document the change.
286	- Bootstrap order surprises (e.g., `&&` needs `if`, but `if`
287	  needs `0=` from `prim/`, etc.). Resolve by writing the load
288	  order BEFORE writing definitions.
289	
290	### 8. `tuplet-demos`
291	
292	**Goal.** Example gallery proving the language works in
293	practice. Six to ten `.tup` programs covering tuple decls,
294	destructuring, multi-output verbs, call-with-splice,
295	control-flow, and at least one program exercising the COR24
296	LED via a prelude-defined wrapper.
297	
298	**Entrance criteria.**
299	- `tuplet-prelude` complete.
300	
301	**Exit criteria.**
302	- 6-10 demos under `demos/*.tup`, each with a reg-rs baseline
303	  in `work/reg-rs/tuplet_demo_<name>.rgt` + `*.out`.
304	- Every demo runs via `tuplet run demos/<name>.tup` and
305	  produces stable UART output.
306	- A "negative" reg-rs test removes `lib/std.tup` and verifies
307	  representative demos *fail to parse* -- proving the prelude
308	  is load-bearing, not duplicated in the compiler.
309	- A README under `demos/` listing each demo with one-line
310	  description.
311	
312	**Key deliverables.**
313	- `demos/coord2.tup` -- tuple init + destructure.
314	- `demos/divmod.tup` -- `div2` use.
315	- `demos/sort_pair.tup` -- `max2` / `min2`.
316	- `demos/branch.tup` -- prelude `if/then/else`.
317	- `demos/loop.tup` -- prelude `while`.
318	- `demos/plot.tup` -- call-with-splice into `plot`.
319	- `demos/led.tup` -- COR24 LED via a `set_led` wrapper from a
320	  prelude addendum.
321	- `demos/README.md` listing all of the above.
322	
323	**Primary risks.**
324	- LED demo requires Forth runtime words for COR24 board I/O
325	  that may need confirmation in `sw-cor24-forth`; verify
326	  before committing to the demo.
327	- Demo audience drift -- keep them tight, focused on language
328	  features, not contrived puzzles.
329	
330	## Out-of-scope (still)
331	
332	Per `docs/prd.md` non-goals; not revisited in this plan:
333	
334	- Hindley-Milner type inference; no static types beyond arity.
335	- Modules, functors, namespaces (the `prim/` and `std/`
336	  prefixes are *naming convention*, not a module system).
337	- Exceptions, effects.
338	- GC heap; values are stack cells.
339	- Floating point beyond percent literals.
340	- Self-hosting parser (parser stays in OCaml; revisit
341	  post-PoC).
342	
343	## Future work (Phase 9+, not planned)
344	
345	- Mixfix precedence levels (Agda-style) replacing
346	  longest-match.
347	- Hygienic macros.
348	- Self-hosting Tuplet parser written in Tuplet.
349	- Type system over arity.
350

# Tuplet -- Phased Plan

> **Architecture source of truth:** `docs/kernel.md`. The
> kernel/prelude split decided there shapes every saga below.
> If this plan disagrees with `docs/kernel.md`, the kernel doc
> wins; raise the disagreement and update this file.
>
> **PoC milestone:** `docs/poc-goals.md` defines the single
> demoable target for this project: a REPL where the user types
> a `syntax` declaration that adds `do..while` and uses it. The
> milestone lands inside saga 6 (`tuplet-forth-emit`) plus a
> small REPL driver -- *not* at the end of saga 8. Sagas 7-8
> exist to thicken the language; the PoC proves the mechanism.

## Arc summary

`tuplet-scaffold` (Phase 0, in-progress) lays down the docs and
toolchain wiring. From there, eight follow-up sagas build the
PoC end-to-end: lexer -> registry-based parser -> registry-based
checker -> stack IR -> reference interpreter -> Forth emitter ->
**`lib/std.tup` prelude** -> demos. The seventh saga
(`tuplet-prelude`) is what makes Tuplet "mostly written in
itself"; the eighth proves it by running demos that depend on
the prelude.

## Saga index

| #  | Saga                          | Phase | One-line goal                                                                | Status        |
|----|-------------------------------|-------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| 0  | `tuplet-scaffold`             | 0     | Docs (PRD, grammar, design, lowering, kernel) + toolchain smoke baselines.   | in-progress   |
| 1  | `tuplet-lexer`                | 1     | Tokenize `.tup`; surface registry-callback for template literals.            | upcoming      |
| 2  | `tuplet-parser`               | 2     | `syntax` registry + longest-match template matcher producing AST.            | upcoming      |
| 3  | `tuplet-checker`              | 3     | Name resolution + arity check against the registry; no hardcoded ops.        | upcoming      |
| 4  | `tuplet-ir`                   | 4     | AST -> stack IR including `IPrimForth` and anonymous-verb thunks.            | upcoming      |
| 5  | `tuplet-interp`               | 5     | Minimal reference interpreter over IR; oracle for emitter cross-checks.      | upcoming      |
| 6  | `tuplet-forth-emit`           | 6     | IR -> Forth into `work/generated/*.fs`; cor24-run round-trip via reg-rs.     | upcoming      |
| 7  | **`tuplet-prelude`**          | 7     | Write `lib/std.tup`: operators + control flow + tuple helpers in Tuplet.     | upcoming      |
| 8  | `tuplet-demos`                | 8     | Example gallery using prelude features only; "remove std.tup -> fail" test.  | upcoming      |

## Cross-cutting concerns

Every saga must honor these. They are not restated in each saga
section.

- **Do not edit outside this repo.** Tuplet code, docs, tests,
  generated Forth, and reg-rs baselines all live in
  `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tuplet`.
- **Upstream gaps are GitHub issues, not local workarounds.**
  Missing features or bugs in `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml` or
  `sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth` are filed via `gh issue create
  --repo sw-embed/<repo>`. Record the issue number under the
  saga's risks. If it blocks the saga, run `agentrail abort
  --reason "blocked on sw-embed/<repo>#<n>"`.
- **Push every commit in the same session.** Per CLAUDE.md
  section 4. Never defer `git push`.
- **Commit the full `.agentrail/` delta with each step.** Stage
  everything modified or new under `.agentrail/`, not just the
  step you thought you were touching.
- **`markdown-checker` clean on every changed `.md`.**
- **reg-rs baseline policy.** `*.rgt` (test definition) and
  `*.out` (golden output) are tracked; `*.tdb` and `*.lock` are
  ignored. New tests live under `work/reg-rs/` via
  `REG_RS_DATA_DIR=work/reg-rs reg-rs create ...`.

## Per-saga sections

### 1. `tuplet-lexer`

**Goal.** Tokenize Tuplet source. Hand-written lexer in the
OCaml subset hosted on `sw-cor24-ocaml`. Surfaces an interface
the parser uses to register new literal tokens during parsing
(needed because `syntax` declarations introduce new keywords).

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-scaffold` complete on `main`.
- `docs/grammar.md`, `docs/kernel.md` present.
- `tuplet_ocaml_smoke` reg-rs test green.

**Exit criteria.**
- `src/lexer.ml` (or equivalent path) tokenizes a `.tup` file
  to a list of tokens, dumpable as a deterministic line per
  token.
- ASCII and Unicode aliases per `docs/grammar.md` table both
  produce the same token stream.
- Registry-callback hook present: parser can call
  `Lexer.add_literal "if"` and a subsequent re-lex of the same
  source treats `if` as a literal token.
- Reg-rs baselines for: scalar literals, tuple-var names with
  arity suffixes, comments, ASCII vs Unicode form, dynamic
  registration round-trip.

**Key deliverables.**
- `src/lexer.ml`, `src/token.ml`.
- `tests/lexer/*.tup` + `*.expected.tokens`.
- `work/reg-rs/tuplet_lex_*.rgt` + `*.out`.

**Primary risks.**
- OCaml subset may lack efficient string compare or mutable
  hashtables for the literal registry. File upstream issue
  against `sw-embed/sw-cor24-ocaml` if so; fall back to assoc
  lists in the meantime.
- Unicode multi-byte handling. Strategy: keep alias folding
  byte-pattern based, narrow set, ASCII-canonical post-fold.

### 2. `tuplet-parser`

**Goal.** Build AST per `docs/design.md`, with a `syntax`
registry and longest-match template matcher. The parser knows
no operators or control-flow keywords -- those come from the
registry.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-lexer` complete; lexer can register literal tokens.
- `docs/kernel.md` `syntax` semantics accepted.

**Exit criteria.**
- `src/parser.ml` parses kernel forms (`syntax`, `:`, `<-`,
  comma, parens, `#`, `_`, `{}`, `prim/forth`) without any
  registry entries.
- After processing one `syntax` declaration, subsequent code
  that matches the template parses to the documented AST.
- Longest-match-wins, first-declared-wins-on-ties: tested with
  a fixture that registers two overlapping templates.
- AST-dump format is deterministic (s-expr or similar).
- Reg-rs baselines for: kernel-only programs, template
  expansion sanity, ambiguity resolution, error messages on
  unknown tokens / unmatched templates.

**Key deliverables.**
- `src/parser.ml`, `src/ast.ml`, `src/registry.ml`.
- `tests/parser/*.tup` + `*.expected.ast`.

**Primary risks.**
- Template matching in the OCaml subset may need data
  structures (priority queue / trie) absent from the host. Plan
  for assoc-list fallback; profile only if needed.
- Registry mutation during parse complicates error recovery.
  Keep parser single-pass; on error, abort and report.

### 3. `tuplet-checker`

**Goal.** Name resolution and arity checking on the registry-based
AST. No hardcoded operators; every check goes through the
registry.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-parser` complete; AST + registry stable.

**Exit criteria.**
- All Arity rules from `docs/grammar.md` enforced for forms
  defined in the kernel + any registered `syntax` declarations.
- Pass-case fixtures produce a typed-AST dump; fail-case
  fixtures produce the documented error message text.
- Reg-rs baselines for both pass and fail cases.

**Key deliverables.**
- `src/checker.ml`.
- `tests/checker/pass/*.tup`, `tests/checker/fail/*.tup` +
  `*.expected.err`.

**Primary risks.**
- Slot-arity propagation through nested templates; document
  the algorithm in a short note alongside the implementation.

### 4. `tuplet-ir`

**Goal.** Lower checked AST to the stack IR from
`docs/design.md`, extended with `IPrimForth` and anonymous-verb
thunks per `docs/kernel.md`.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-checker` complete.

**Exit criteria.**
- Every IR opcode from the design doc emitted correctly,
  including `IPrimForth "WORD"` for raw escape and synthetic
  thunks for `{...}`.
- Reg-rs baselines for the IR dumps of representative programs
  (kernel-only, with prelude templates expanded).

**Key deliverables.**
- `src/ir.ml`, `src/lower.ml`.
- `tests/ir/*.tup` + `*.expected.ir`.

**Primary risks.**
- IR ergonomics for thunks. `:NONAME` is now available
  (`sw-embed/sw-cor24-forth#5` closed), so thunks lower
  directly without the synthetic-name fallback originally
  planned.

### 5. `tuplet-interp`

**Goal.** A minimal reference interpreter over the IR. Used as
an oracle for the Forth emitter -- not a target itself.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-ir` complete.

**Exit criteria.**
- For every program in the test corpus, the interpreter
  produces the same output the Forth emitter will be expected
  to produce.
- Stub for `IPrimForth`: a small allowlist of supported words
  (`+`, `*`, `<`, `IF`, etc.) implemented in the interpreter
  with mock semantics.

**Key deliverables.**
- `src/interp.ml`.
- `tests/interp/*.tup` + `*.expected.out`.

**Primary risks.**
- Drift between interpreter and Forth semantics for edge
  cases; document divergences explicitly. The Forth output is
  authoritative; the interpreter is the cross-check.

### 6. `tuplet-forth-emit`

**Goal.** Emit Forth from IR per `docs/lowering.md`, into
`work/generated/<basename>.fs`. Concatenate kernel `forth.s` +
generated file, run under `cor24-run`, capture UART output via
reg-rs, compare against the interpreter. **PoC milestone lands
here**: the saga's exit also delivers a `tuplet repl` driver
sufficient to demonstrate `docs/poc-goals.md`.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-ir` and `tuplet-interp` complete.
- `tuplet_forth_smoke` reg-rs test green.

**Exit criteria.**
- `tuplet compile <file>.tup` writes `work/generated/<file>.fs`.
- `tuplet run <file>.tup` runs end-to-end through `cor24-run`
  and prints the UART output section.
- For every program in the test corpus, interpreter output
  matches Forth output (modulo the documented divergence
  policy).
- Reg-rs baselines compare emitter output against the
  interpreter.

**Key deliverables.**
- `src/emit.ml`.
- `bin/tuplet` (the CLI; `tuplet compile`, `tuplet run`,
  `tuplet repl`).
- `work/generated/` (gitignored output dir).
- `work/reg-rs/tuplet_poc_dowhile.{rgt,out}` -- scripted REPL
  session per `docs/poc-goals.md` acceptance criteria.
- `docs/poc-demo.md` -- the PoC walkthrough.

**Primary risks.**
- Per-instruction RX/TX trace lines from `cor24-run` shifting
  with emulator version; existing grep-preprocess pattern from
  `tuplet_forth_smoke` is the template.
- Anonymous verbs use kernel `:NONAME` (verified via
  `tuplet_forth_noname_smoke`).

### 7. `tuplet-prelude` (the bootstrap-proof saga)

**Goal.** Write `lib/std.tup`: every operator (`+ - * < = <> <=
>= not && ||`), every multi-output verb (`max min div max2 min2
div2`), and every control-flow construct (`if/then/else/end`,
`while/do/end`, sketch of `match`) defined in Tuplet using only
the kernel + earlier prelude entries + `prim/forth` escapes.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-forth-emit` complete; round-trip via reg-rs working.

**Exit criteria.**
- `lib/std.tup` exists and loads without forward-reference
  errors.
- A test program using `+`, `if/then/else/end`, `while/do/end`
  produces the expected output via the Tuplet emitter, using
  *only* prelude features for those constructs.
- The prelude file has a header comment listing the load
  order; a build-time check fails on forward references.

**Key deliverables.**
- `lib/std.tup`.
- `tests/prelude/*.tup` exercising every prelude form.
- A loader / driver that wires `lib/std.tup` into the
  compiler's startup sequence.

**Primary risks.**
- The litmus test in `docs/kernel.md` is wrong somewhere. If a
  prelude entry needs a primitive that isn't in `prim/`, that's
  evidence the kernel inventory needs a new entry; revisit
  `docs/kernel.md` and document the change.
- Bootstrap order surprises (e.g., `&&` needs `if`, but `if`
  needs `0=` from `prim/`, etc.). Resolve by writing the load
  order BEFORE writing definitions.

### 8. `tuplet-demos`

**Goal.** Example gallery proving the language works in
practice. Six to ten `.tup` programs covering tuple decls,
destructuring, multi-output verbs, call-with-splice,
control-flow, and at least one program exercising the COR24
LED via a prelude-defined wrapper.

**Entrance criteria.**
- `tuplet-prelude` complete.

**Exit criteria.**
- 6-10 demos under `demos/*.tup`, each with a reg-rs baseline
  in `work/reg-rs/tuplet_demo_<name>.rgt` + `*.out`.
- Every demo runs via `tuplet run demos/<name>.tup` and
  produces stable UART output.
- A "negative" reg-rs test removes `lib/std.tup` and verifies
  representative demos *fail to parse* -- proving the prelude
  is load-bearing, not duplicated in the compiler.
- A README under `demos/` listing each demo with one-line
  description.

**Key deliverables.**
- `demos/coord2.tup` -- tuple init + destructure.
- `demos/divmod.tup` -- `div2` use.
- `demos/sort_pair.tup` -- `max2` / `min2`.
- `demos/branch.tup` -- prelude `if/then/else`.
- `demos/loop.tup` -- prelude `while`.
- `demos/plot.tup` -- call-with-splice into `plot`.
- `demos/led.tup` -- COR24 LED via a `set_led` wrapper from a
  prelude addendum.
- `demos/README.md` listing all of the above.

**Primary risks.**
- LED demo requires Forth runtime words for COR24 board I/O
  that may need confirmation in `sw-cor24-forth`; verify
  before committing to the demo.
- Demo audience drift -- keep them tight, focused on language
  features, not contrived puzzles.

## Out-of-scope (still)

Per `docs/prd.md` non-goals; not revisited in this plan:

- Hindley-Milner type inference; no static types beyond arity.
- Modules, functors, namespaces (the `prim/` and `std/`
  prefixes are *naming convention*, not a module system).
- Exceptions, effects.
- GC heap; values are stack cells.
- Floating point beyond percent literals.
- Self-hosting parser (parser stays in OCaml; revisit
  post-PoC).

## Future work (Phase 9+, not planned)

- Mixfix precedence levels (Agda-style) replacing
  longest-match.
- Hygienic macros.
- Self-hosting Tuplet parser written in Tuplet.
- Type system over arity.

1	type token = THash of int list | TInt of int | TPct of int | TMinus | TLArrow | TRArrow | TLParen | TRParen | TLBrace | TRBrace | TComma | TUnderscore | TMint | TIdent of int list | TUnknown of int | TEOF
2	let rec emit_bytes bs = match bs with [] -> () | h :: t -> let _ = putc h in emit_bytes t
3	let prefix_hash = [72; 65; 83; 72; 32; 32; 32]
4	let prefix_int = [73; 78; 84; 32; 32; 32; 32]
5	let prefix_pct = [80; 67; 84; 32; 32; 32; 32]
6	let prefix_unk = [85; 78; 75; 32; 32; 32; 32]
7	let prefix_id = [73; 68; 69; 78; 84; 32; 32]
8	let rec int_to_bytes_aux x acc = if x = 0 then acc else int_to_bytes_aux (x / 10) ((x mod 10 + 48) :: acc)
9	let int_to_bytes n = if n = 0 then [48] else int_to_bytes_aux n []
10	let dump_tok t = match t with TEOF -> print_endline "EOF" | TMinus -> print_endline "MINUS" | TLArrow -> print_endline "LARROW" | TRArrow -> print_endline "RARROW" | TLParen -> print_endline "LPAREN" | TRParen -> print_endline "RPAREN" | TLBrace -> print_endline "LBRACE" | TRBrace -> print_endline "RBRACE" | TComma -> print_endline "COMMA" | TUnderscore -> print_endline "USCORE" | TMint -> print_endline "MINT" | THash bs -> let _ = emit_bytes prefix_hash in let _ = emit_bytes bs in putc 10 | TInt n -> let _ = emit_bytes prefix_int in let _ = emit_bytes (int_to_bytes n) in putc 10 | TPct n -> let _ = emit_bytes prefix_pct in let _ = emit_bytes (int_to_bytes n) in putc 10 | TIdent bs -> let _ = emit_bytes prefix_id in let _ = emit_bytes bs in putc 10 | TUnknown b -> let _ = emit_bytes prefix_unk in let _ = print_int b in putc 10
11	let rec dump_tokens toks = match toks with [] -> () | h :: t -> let _ = dump_tok h in dump_tokens t
12	let is_ws c = c = 32 || c = 9 || c = 10 || c = 13
13	let is_digit c = c >= 48 && c <= 57
14	let is_letter c = (c >= 65 && c <= 90) || (c >= 97 && c <= 122)
15	let is_ident_cont c = is_letter c || is_digit c || c = 95
16	let rec collect_comment acc = let c = getc () in if c = 10 then List.rev acc else if c = 3 then List.rev acc else collect_comment (c :: acc)
17	let rec lex_digits n = let b = getc () in if is_digit b then lex_digits (n * 10 + (b - 48)) else (n, b)
18	let rec lex_ident_body acc = let b = getc () in if is_ident_cont b then lex_ident_body (b :: acc) else (acc, b)
19	let lex_ident_after start acc = let pair = lex_ident_body start in let rev_body = fst pair in let next = snd pair in if next = 63 then (TIdent (List.rev (63 :: rev_body)) :: acc, 0) else (TIdent (List.rev rev_body) :: acc, next)
20	let lex_uscore acc = let nxt = getc () in if is_ident_cont nxt then lex_ident_after [nxt; 95] acc else (TUnderscore :: acc, nxt)
21	let lex_lt acc = let nxt = getc () in if nxt = 45 then (TLArrow :: acc, 0) else (TUnknown 60 :: acc, nxt)
22	let lex_minus acc = let nxt = getc () in if nxt = 62 then (TRArrow :: acc, 0) else (TMinus :: acc, nxt)
23	let rec lex_loop pre acc = let b = if pre = 0 then getc () else pre in if b = 3 then List.rev (TEOF :: acc) else if is_ws b then lex_loop 0 acc else if b = 35 then let body = collect_comment [] in lex_loop 0 (THash body :: acc) else if b = 60 then let pair = lex_lt acc in lex_loop (snd pair) (fst pair) else if b = 45 then let pair = lex_minus acc in lex_loop (snd pair) (fst pair) else if b = 40 then lex_loop 0 (TLParen :: acc) else if b = 41 then lex_loop 0 (TRParen :: acc) else if b = 123 then lex_loop 0 (TLBrace :: acc) else if b = 125 then lex_loop 0 (TRBrace :: acc) else if b = 44 then lex_loop 0 (TComma :: acc) else if b = 95 then let pair = lex_uscore acc in lex_loop (snd pair) (fst pair) else if b = 42 then lex_loop 0 (TMint :: acc) else if is_digit b then let pair = lex_digits (b - 48) in let n = fst pair in let next = snd pair in if next = 37 then lex_loop 0 (TPct n :: acc) else lex_loop next (TInt n :: acc) else if is_letter b then let pair = lex_ident_after [b] acc in lex_loop (snd pair) (fst pair) else lex_loop 0 (TUnknown b :: acc)
24	let _ = dump_tokens (lex_loop 0 [])

1	# DSL Examples -- What Users Can Build with Tuplet
2	
3	These are aspirational examples of language extensions a DSL
4	author might mint *in Tuplet itself*, after the PoC kernel +
5	prelude exist. They are not part of the PoC scope; they are the
6	**reason** the PoC scope is what it is. If the mechanism the
7	PoC demonstrates can't grow into supporting these, the design
8	is wrong.
9	
10	The PoC ships only the mechanism (`*syntax T expand E`) and one
11	small witness (`do..while`); these examples show how far the
12	mechanism is meant to scale.
13	
14	## Example: user-minted conditional family
15	
16	```
17	# Standard if/then/else, minted in lib/std.tup (NOT in the kernel)
18	*syntax if _ then _ else _ end expand
19	  _1  prim/forth "IF"  _2  prim/forth "ELSE"  _3  prim/forth "THEN"
20	
21	# Approximate-equal conditional with absolute tolerance
22	*syntax if-approx _ approx _ within _ then _ else _ end expand
23	  _1 _2 - abs _3 < if _4 else _5 end
24	
25	# Approximate-equal with percent tolerance
26	*syntax if-approx-pct _ approx _ within _ pct then _ else _ end expand
27	  _1 _2 - abs _2 _3 * 100 div < if _4 else _5 end
28	
29	# Tail-conditional postfix form (math style)
30	*syntax _ if _ else _ expand
31	  _2  prim/forth "IF"  _1  prim/forth "ELSE"  _3  prim/forth "THEN"
32	```
33	
34	Use sites:
35	
36	```
37	classify <- if-approx 10 approx 12 within 3 then close else far end   # -> close
38	classify <- if-approx-pct 100 approx 110 within 5 pct then close else far end   # -> far
39	
40	# Tail conditional, math style
41	result <- N^E if E >= 0 else 0
42	```
43	
44	## Example: user-defined verb with body
45	
46	```
47	# Math-style curried definition
48	*Power N E := N^E if E >= 0 else 0
49	
50	# Standard signature-with-body form
51	*power (n e) -> (p) <- power-body
52	  # body uses minted control flow: do..times etc.
53	```
54	
55	## Example: structured-block iteration (3-piece delimiter)
56	
57	```
58	# A loop construct minted in the prelude. The `{ ... | ... | ... }`
59	# form has three pieces: init / count / step. Modeled on Forth's
60	# DO-LOOP idiom.
61	*syntax { _ | _ | _ } expand
62	  _1  prim/forth "0"  _2  prim/forth "DO"  _3  prim/forth "LOOP"
63	
64	# Use site: power = N^E
65	*power (N E) -> (P) <- { 1 | E | N * }
66	```
67	
68	(The Unicode form would use the long curly-bracket-hook glyphs
69	U+23A7 / U+23A8 / U+23A9 / U+23AB rather than ASCII pipes; the
70	ASCII form here is a placeholder.)
71	
72	## What features these examples imply
73	
74	Each row is a feature the spec touches and a status note. None
75	of these are PoC scope; this is the roadmap for after the PoC
76	demonstrates the mechanism.
77	
78	| Feature                                | Used in              | Status                              |
79	|----------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
80	| `*syntax T expand E` declaration       | every example        | **PoC scope** -- already specified  |
81	| `*name p1 p2 := body` (curried def)    | `Power N E := ...`   | not specified -- needs new form     |
82	| `*name(args) -> (outs) <- body`        | `*power (N E) ...`   | partly specified in `docs/grammar.md` example 5 |
83	| Unicode in identifiers (Greek, math)   | `if<U+2248>`, `<U+03B1>`, `<U+03C1>` | not specified -- needs lexer extension |
84	| Hyphens in identifiers                 | `if-close?`          | not specified -- needs lexer rule   |
85	| `:=` definitional vs `<-` assignment   | `Power N E := ...`   | not specified -- decide if synonym  |
86	| Tail conditional `_ if _ else _`       | `N^E if E >= 0 ...`  | mintable as `*syntax`               |
87	| Approximate-equal operator `~~` / U+2248 | `a ~~ b`           | mintable                            |
88	| Tolerance binder `+/-` / U+00B1        | `a ~~ b +/- e`       | mintable                            |
89	| Type annotations `n : Int` / U+2124    | `(n : Z)`            | not in PoC; future saga             |
90	| Three-piece block `{ _ \| _ \| _ }`    | power body           | mintable                            |
91	| Test-arrow `->>` / U+2192              | `Power 2 10 ->> 1024`| mintable as a top-level assertion form |
92	| Runtime assertion / guard              | `iff e is positive`  | needs runtime assert primitive (`prim/forth "ABORT"`) |
93	| Exponentiation `^`                     | `N^E`                | mintable as `*syntax _ ^ _ expand ...` |
94	
95	## Open design questions
96	
97	The examples surface real choices that haven't been made yet.
98	Listing them so they're not silently picked by the parser
99	implementation:
100	
101	1. **`if/then/else` location: kernel or prelude?**
102	   Current spec puts it in `lib/std.tup` (prelude). The user's
103	   note "I want to be able to mint if/then/else" confirms this:
104	   even `if/then/else` is library, not language. **Decision:
105	   no change; `if/then/else` stays in the prelude.**
106	
107	2. **`:=` vs `<-`: distinct operators or synonyms?**
108	   `<-` is ordinary assignment to an existing name (the kernel
109	   form). `:=` is used in math-style definitions (`Power N E
110	   := ...`). Two reasonable options:
111	   - (a) Synonyms; document `:=` as a Unicode-friendly alias.
112	   - (b) Distinct: `<-` is "store now," `:=` is "bind name to
113	     expression / verb body" (definitional, like Coq's `Definition`).
114	   **Awaiting decision.**
115	
116	3. **Curried `*name p1 p2 := body` form:**
117	   This is a third declaration form, distinct from the existing
118	   `*name -> (outs)` (tuple-var) and `*name(ins) -> (outs) <- body`
119	   (verb signature with body). Is it sugar for the explicit form,
120	   or a peer? **Awaiting decision.**
121	
122	4. **Unicode and hyphen in identifiers:**
123	   Currently, identifiers are `[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\??`.
124	   Examples need:
125	   - Greek letters and math glyphs in the middle of identifiers
126	     (e.g. `if<U+2248>`).
127	   - Hyphen-minus inside identifiers (e.g. `if-close?`).
128	   Both are pure lexer extensions. **Likely both yes**, but each
129	   requires careful disambiguation (hyphen vs subtraction).
130	   **Awaiting decision.**
131	
132	5. **Type annotations `n : Z`:**
133	   The PoC has no type system. Annotations like `(n : Z e : Z)`
134	   are decorative for the PoC at most. Either reject them at the
135	   parser (PoC), or accept-and-discard. **Decision: reject in
136	   PoC; queue for a future typing saga.**
137	
138	6. **Three-piece block `{ _ | _ | _ }`:**
139	   Two-piece blocks `{ _ }` are anonymous-verb literals (kernel).
140	   Three-piece blocks could be:
141	   - (a) Mintable as a `*syntax` declaration (preferred -- stays
142	     consistent with the everything-is-extensions principle).
143	   - (b) A new kernel form.
144	   **Decision pending; preference (a).**
145	
146	7. **Test-arrow / assertion form `->>` / U+2192:**
147	   `Power 2 10 ->> 1024` is a test assertion. Mintable as a
148	   `*syntax expr ->> expected expand ...` that compiles to "run
149	   expr, compare, abort if mismatch, otherwise continue."
150	   **Mintable; not PoC scope.**
151	
152	8. **Runtime assertions:**
153	   `Power 5 -3 ->> assertion failed` requires a runtime ABORT
154	   (which `sw-cor24-forth` provides via `QUIT` / `ABORT`).
155	   Wrap as `prim/forth "ABORT"` in a new prelude verb `assert`.
156	   **Mintable once the prelude exists.**
157	
158	## What the PoC actually proves
159	
160	The PoC's `do..while` demo is a **two-slot** `*syntax`
161	declaration with `expand`. That is the same mechanism as every
162	example in this file. If `do..while` works end-to-end (REPL +
163	emit + run + reg-rs baseline), the mechanism is proven; the
164	examples in this file are then "just more `*syntax`
165	declarations" to write in the prelude saga and beyond.
166	
167	The PoC milestone explicitly DOES NOT ship:
168	
169	- `if/then/else` (prelude saga 7)
170	- Any of the conditional families above
171	- Curried `:=` definitions
172	- Unicode identifiers
173	- Hyphenated identifiers
174	- Type annotations
175	- Three-piece blocks
176	- Test-arrow assertions
177	
178	It does ship: the mechanism that lets a user write all of those
179	in `lib/std.tup` or in their own files, without ever touching
180	the compiler.
181	
182	## Reference
183	
184	- `docs/poc-goals.md` -- the PoC milestone scope.
185	- `docs/kernel.md` -- the kernel/prelude split: what is and
186	  isn't mintable.
187	- `docs/grammar.md` -- current surface grammar (subset of what
188	  these examples eventually need).
189	- `docs/plan.md` -- the saga arc; sagas 7 (prelude) and 8
190	  (demos) absorb most of this file's examples.
191

# DSL Examples -- What Users Can Build with Tuplet

These are aspirational examples of language extensions a DSL
author might mint *in Tuplet itself*, after the PoC kernel +
prelude exist. They are not part of the PoC scope; they are the
**reason** the PoC scope is what it is. If the mechanism the
PoC demonstrates can't grow into supporting these, the design
is wrong.

The PoC ships only the mechanism (`*syntax T expand E`) and one
small witness (`do..while`); these examples show how far the
mechanism is meant to scale.

## Example: user-minted conditional family

```
# Standard if/then/else, minted in lib/std.tup (NOT in the kernel)
*syntax if _ then _ else _ end expand
  _1  prim/forth "IF"  _2  prim/forth "ELSE"  _3  prim/forth "THEN"

# Approximate-equal conditional with absolute tolerance
*syntax if-approx _ approx _ within _ then _ else _ end expand
  _1 _2 - abs _3 < if _4 else _5 end

# Approximate-equal with percent tolerance
*syntax if-approx-pct _ approx _ within _ pct then _ else _ end expand
  _1 _2 - abs _2 _3 * 100 div < if _4 else _5 end

# Tail-conditional postfix form (math style)
*syntax _ if _ else _ expand
  _2  prim/forth "IF"  _1  prim/forth "ELSE"  _3  prim/forth "THEN"
```

Use sites:

```
classify <- if-approx 10 approx 12 within 3 then close else far end   # -> close
classify <- if-approx-pct 100 approx 110 within 5 pct then close else far end   # -> far

# Tail conditional, math style
result <- N^E if E >= 0 else 0
```

## Example: user-defined verb with body

```
# Math-style curried definition
*Power N E := N^E if E >= 0 else 0

# Standard signature-with-body form
*power (n e) -> (p) <- power-body
  # body uses minted control flow: do..times etc.
```

## Example: structured-block iteration (3-piece delimiter)

```
# A loop construct minted in the prelude. The `{ ... | ... | ... }`
# form has three pieces: init / count / step. Modeled on Forth's
# DO-LOOP idiom.
*syntax { _ | _ | _ } expand
  _1  prim/forth "0"  _2  prim/forth "DO"  _3  prim/forth "LOOP"

# Use site: power = N^E
*power (N E) -> (P) <- { 1 | E | N * }
```

(The Unicode form would use the long curly-bracket-hook glyphs
U+23A7 / U+23A8 / U+23A9 / U+23AB rather than ASCII pipes; the
ASCII form here is a placeholder.)

## What features these examples imply

Each row is a feature the spec touches and a status note. None
of these are PoC scope; this is the roadmap for after the PoC
demonstrates the mechanism.

| Feature                                | Used in              | Status                              |
|----------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| `*syntax T expand E` declaration       | every example        | **PoC scope** -- already specified  |
| `*name p1 p2 := body` (curried def)    | `Power N E := ...`   | not specified -- needs new form     |
| `*name(args) -> (outs) <- body`        | `*power (N E) ...`   | partly specified in `docs/grammar.md` example 5 |
| Unicode in identifiers (Greek, math)   | `if<U+2248>`, `<U+03B1>`, `<U+03C1>` | not specified -- needs lexer extension |
| Hyphens in identifiers                 | `if-close?`          | not specified -- needs lexer rule   |
| `:=` definitional vs `<-` assignment   | `Power N E := ...`   | not specified -- decide if synonym  |
| Tail conditional `_ if _ else _`       | `N^E if E >= 0 ...`  | mintable as `*syntax`               |
| Approximate-equal operator `~~` / U+2248 | `a ~~ b`           | mintable                            |
| Tolerance binder `+/-` / U+00B1        | `a ~~ b +/- e`       | mintable                            |
| Type annotations `n : Int` / U+2124    | `(n : Z)`            | not in PoC; future saga             |
| Three-piece block `{ _ \| _ \| _ }`    | power body           | mintable                            |
| Test-arrow `->>` / U+2192              | `Power 2 10 ->> 1024`| mintable as a top-level assertion form |
| Runtime assertion / guard              | `iff e is positive`  | needs runtime assert primitive (`prim/forth "ABORT"`) |
| Exponentiation `^`                     | `N^E`                | mintable as `*syntax _ ^ _ expand ...` |

## Open design questions

The examples surface real choices that haven't been made yet.
Listing them so they're not silently picked by the parser
implementation:

1. **`if/then/else` location: kernel or prelude?**
   Current spec puts it in `lib/std.tup` (prelude). The user's
   note "I want to be able to mint if/then/else" confirms this:
   even `if/then/else` is library, not language. **Decision:
   no change; `if/then/else` stays in the prelude.**

2. **`:=` vs `<-`: distinct operators or synonyms?**
   `<-` is ordinary assignment to an existing name (the kernel
   form). `:=` is used in math-style definitions (`Power N E
   := ...`). Two reasonable options:
   - (a) Synonyms; document `:=` as a Unicode-friendly alias.
   - (b) Distinct: `<-` is "store now," `:=` is "bind name to
     expression / verb body" (definitional, like Coq's `Definition`).
   **Awaiting decision.**

3. **Curried `*name p1 p2 := body` form:**
   This is a third declaration form, distinct from the existing
   `*name -> (outs)` (tuple-var) and `*name(ins) -> (outs) <- body`
   (verb signature with body). Is it sugar for the explicit form,
   or a peer? **Awaiting decision.**

4. **Unicode and hyphen in identifiers:**
   Currently, identifiers are `[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_]*\??`.
   Examples need:
   - Greek letters and math glyphs in the middle of identifiers
     (e.g. `if<U+2248>`).
   - Hyphen-minus inside identifiers (e.g. `if-close?`).
   Both are pure lexer extensions. **Likely both yes**, but each
   requires careful disambiguation (hyphen vs subtraction).
   **Awaiting decision.**

5. **Type annotations `n : Z`:**
   The PoC has no type system. Annotations like `(n : Z e : Z)`
   are decorative for the PoC at most. Either reject them at the
   parser (PoC), or accept-and-discard. **Decision: reject in
   PoC; queue for a future typing saga.**

6. **Three-piece block `{ _ | _ | _ }`:**
   Two-piece blocks `{ _ }` are anonymous-verb literals (kernel).
   Three-piece blocks could be:
   - (a) Mintable as a `*syntax` declaration (preferred -- stays
     consistent with the everything-is-extensions principle).
   - (b) A new kernel form.
   **Decision pending; preference (a).**

7. **Test-arrow / assertion form `->>` / U+2192:**
   `Power 2 10 ->> 1024` is a test assertion. Mintable as a
   `*syntax expr ->> expected expand ...` that compiles to "run
   expr, compare, abort if mismatch, otherwise continue."
   **Mintable; not PoC scope.**

8. **Runtime assertions:**
   `Power 5 -3 ->> assertion failed` requires a runtime ABORT
   (which `sw-cor24-forth` provides via `QUIT` / `ABORT`).
   Wrap as `prim/forth "ABORT"` in a new prelude verb `assert`.
   **Mintable once the prelude exists.**

## What the PoC actually proves

The PoC's `do..while` demo is a **two-slot** `*syntax`
declaration with `expand`. That is the same mechanism as every
example in this file. If `do..while` works end-to-end (REPL +
emit + run + reg-rs baseline), the mechanism is proven; the
examples in this file are then "just more `*syntax`
declarations" to write in the prelude saga and beyond.

The PoC milestone explicitly DOES NOT ship:

- `if/then/else` (prelude saga 7)
- Any of the conditional families above
- Curried `:=` definitions
- Unicode identifiers
- Hyphenated identifiers
- Type annotations
- Three-piece blocks
- Test-arrow assertions

It does ship: the mechanism that lets a user write all of those
in `lib/std.tup` or in their own files, without ever touching
the compiler.

## Reference

- `docs/poc-goals.md` -- the PoC milestone scope.
- `docs/kernel.md` -- the kernel/prelude split: what is and
  isn't mintable.
- `docs/grammar.md` -- current surface grammar (subset of what
  these examples eventually need).
- `docs/plan.md` -- the saga arc; sagas 7 (prelude) and 8
  (demos) absorb most of this file's examples.

1	# Tuplet PoC Goals
2	
3	The single demonstrable artifact this project is aiming at.
4	
5	## North Star
6	
7	A **demoable REPL** running a stripped-down Tuplet that lets a
8	user, in a single session, **define a new control-flow construct
9	in Tuplet itself** and then use it. The new construct must fit
10	the language's left-and-right-arrow paradigm (`<-` assignment,
11	`->` mapping arrow) and must be added via the `syntax`
12	declaration form -- no compiler change required.
13	
14	Concretely:
15	
16	```
17	$ tuplet repl
18	> *n <- 0                                  # mint n; initialize
19	> *syntax do _ while _ end expand
20	.   prim/forth "BEGIN"  _1  _2  prim/forth "UNTIL"
21	> do
22	.   n <- n + 1                             # ordinary assign (no mint)
23	.   n prim/forth "."
24	.   prim/forth "SPACE"
25	. while  n < 3  end
26	1 2 3
27	> n
28	3
29	> #
30	```
31	
32	The `*` (canonical Unicode BULLET U+2022; alias BLACK SMALL
33	SQUARE U+25AA) is the **mint operator** -- the user reaches
34	for it whenever they add a new name or syntax to the language.
35	Once the mint operator and the block delimiters (`{ }`, with
36	Unicode aliases U+23A7/U+23AB) exist, nothing else in the
37	language is privileged; everything else is user-extensible.
38	
39	The transcript above is the **acceptance scenario**. If the REPL
40	can produce that output, with the user typing all of it
41	(including the `syntax` declaration), the PoC is met.
42	
43	## What the PoC actually proves
44	
45	Even `if/then/else` is meant to be **user-minted**, not built
46	in -- it lives in `lib/std.tup` (the prelude, saga 7) as a
47	`*syntax` declaration, not in the compiler. The same is true
48	for `while`, `match`, every operator, and every conditional
49	family. The PoC demonstrates the **mechanism** by exercising
50	`do..while`; once the mechanism works, sealed conditionals
51	like `if/then/else` and user-defined variants
52	(`if-approx`, `if-close?`, math-style `_ if _ else _`)
53	all become "just more `*syntax`" entries in `.tup` files.
54	
55	See `docs/dsl-examples.md` for a catalog of the kinds of
56	extensions DSL users are expected to write, and the design
57	questions those examples surface.
58	
59	## Why `do..while` and not `if..then..else`
60	
61	`do..while` is a strictly smaller demo target than
62	`if..then..else`:
63	
64	| Construct                | Slots | Branches at runtime | Forth lowering        |
65	|--------------------------|-------|---------------------|-----------------------|
66	| `if _ then _ else _ end` | 3     | 2 (then or else)    | `IF`, `ELSE`, `THEN`  |
67	| `do _ while _ end`       | 2     | 1 (top of loop)     | `BEGIN`, `UNTIL`      |
68	
69	Two slots vs three, one branch target vs two. Same machinery in
70	both cases (`syntax T expand E` + `prim/forth` escape), and
71	nothing in the kernel needs to know the construct exists. The
72	PoC ships only the smaller of the two.
73	
74	`if..then..else` and `while..do..end` arrive in the prelude
75	saga (see `docs/plan.md` saga 7) once the architecture is
76	proven on the smaller case.
77	
78	## Stripped-down Tuplet for the PoC REPL
79	
80	Only the subset needed for the acceptance scenario above. Every
81	form below has spec text in the existing scaffold docs.
82	
83	| Form                              | Why it's needed                                      |
84	|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
85	| **Mint operator `*`** (Unicode U+2022) | Every new binding (`*n <- 0`, `*syntax ...`).   |
86	| Integer literals                  | The `0`, `1`, `3` in the scenario.                   |
87	| Identifier (ASCII letters + digits) | Variable name `n`.                                 |
88	| Scalar mint+init `*name <- expr`  | First binding of a name with initial value.          |
89	| Scalar assignment `name <- expr`  | Updates to an already-minted name (`n <- n + 1`).    |
90	| Binary `+` and `<`                | Arithmetic and the loop test.                        |
91	| Bare expression statement         | The `n` line at the end (print scalar).              |
92	| `prim/forth "WORD"` raw escape    | Used inside the `syntax` expansion.                  |
93	| `*syntax T expand E` declaration  | The whole point of the demo.                         |
94	| Block delimiters `{` `}`          | Multi-statement loop body (the `do ... end` block).  |
95	| Line comment `#`                  | Quality-of-life only.                                |
96	
97	Everything else from `docs/grammar.md` is **out of scope for the
98	PoC milestone**, including:
99	
100	- Tuple variable declarations (`coord2 -> (x y)`).
101	- Multi-output verbs (`max2`, `min2`, `div2`).
102	- Call-with-splice (`plot(coord2 Red 50%)`).
103	- Anonymous verb literals `{...}`.
104	- Percent literals (`50%`).
105	- Most Unicode aliases (ASCII surface only for the PoC; Unicode
106	  arrives later as a pure lexer extension).
107	- The full builtin verb registry.
108	- LED / board I/O.
109	
110	These are real language features and will arrive in their own
111	sagas, but none of them are gated by the PoC milestone.
112	
113	## When the PoC milestone is met (in plan terms)
114	
115	The PoC is a **vertical slice** through the existing eight-saga
116	arc, not a separate saga. It is met when:
117	
118	- `tuplet-lexer` recognizes the stripped-down token set above,
119	  including dynamic literal registration.
120	- `tuplet-parser` can parse a `syntax` declaration, register the
121	  template, and parse subsequent input that matches it.
122	- `tuplet-checker` arity-checks the registered template (each
123	  slot has output arity 1; whole template has output arity 0).
124	- `tuplet-ir` lowers `do..while` use sites via the registered
125	  expansion.
126	- `tuplet-forth-emit` round-trips the IR through `cor24-run` and
127	  produces the expected UART output.
128	- A REPL driver (a small addition during or after
129	  `tuplet-forth-emit`) reads stdin one logical statement at a
130	  time, compiles, runs, and prints output.
131	
132	We do **not** need:
133	
134	- The full prelude (`tuplet-prelude` saga). For the PoC, the
135	  user types the `syntax` declaration directly, demonstrating
136	  the mechanism. A pre-loaded prelude is a polish step.
137	- Anonymous verb literals. The `expand` form does textual
138	  substitution -- no thunks, no xts, no `:NONAME`.
139	- The demo gallery (`tuplet-demos` saga). One scenario in a
140	  README is enough for the PoC.
141	
142	This means the PoC milestone lands at the **end of saga 6
143	(`tuplet-forth-emit`)** plus a small REPL driver, **not** at
144	the end of saga 8.
145	
146	## REPL behavior
147	
148	For the PoC, the REPL keeps it simple. Each user input is one
149	logical statement (terminated by newline at outer indent).
150	Multi-line constructs use a continuation prompt (`. `) until the
151	opening token sees its matching close.
152	
153	State persistence between inputs: scalar variable values and the
154	syntax registry survive across inputs. The simplest
155	implementation:
156	
157	1. Maintain a Tuplet-side environment (variable->value map, the
158	   syntax registry) inside the OCaml-subset REPL process.
159	2. Each input compiles to a fresh Forth program: the kernel
160	   prelude + reconstructed scalar `CREATE`s + the new statement.
161	3. Run that program through `cor24-run` and capture the output
162	   delta from the previous run.
163	
164	This is wasteful per input but correct, requires nothing from
165	the runtime beyond what the existing smoke tests already use,
166	and is good enough for the PoC. Optimizing to a persistent
167	`cor24-run` is a follow-up.
168	
169	## Acceptance criteria
170	
171	The PoC is met when **all** of the following hold:
172	
173	1. `tuplet repl` (or equivalent invocation) starts a REPL.
174	2. The exact transcript above can be reproduced by typing it.
175	   Output bytes match `1 2 3\n3\n` (whitespace exact).
176	3. A reg-rs baseline exercises a scripted REPL session that
177	   defines `do..while` and uses it. The baseline is committed
178	   to `work/reg-rs/tuplet_poc_dowhile.{rgt,out}`.
179	4. A short `docs/poc-demo.md` walks through the scenario with
180	   commentary on what's happening at each step.
181	5. The user can substitute `do..while` for some other
182	   two-slot template (e.g. `unless _ do _ end`) and the REPL
183	   accepts it -- proving the mechanism is general, not
184	   special-cased.
185	
186	Item 5 is the part that proves "the language can add language
187	features." If only `do..while` works and `unless` mysteriously
188	fails, the PoC is a sham.
189	
190	## Out of PoC scope (deferred to later sagas)
191	
192	- `if..then..else` (saga 7 prelude).
193	- `while..do..end` (saga 7 prelude).
194	- `match..with` (saga 7 prelude).
195	- Tuple variables and destructuring (saga 4-7).
196	- Multi-output verbs (saga 4-7).
197	- LED / board I/O demos (saga 8).
198	- Unicode glyph lexing (separate later saga).
199	- Hygiene / name capture (deliberately not in PoC).
200	- Mixfix precedence levels (PoC uses longest-match; document
201	  the corner cases).
